On 11 March 2011, at 14:46:24 [JST](/wiki/Japan_Standard_Time "Japan Standard Time") (05:46:24 [UTC](/wiki/UTC "UTC")), a [Mw](/wiki/Seismic_magnitude_scales#Mw "Seismic magnitude scales") 9.0–9.1 [undersea](/wiki/Submarine_earthquake "Submarine earthquake") [megathrust earthquake](/wiki/Megathrust_earthquake "Megathrust earthquake") occurred in the Pacific Ocean, 72 km (45 mi) east of the [Oshika Peninsula](/wiki/Oshika_Peninsula "Oshika Peninsula") of the [Tōhoku region](/wiki/T%C5%8Dhoku_region "Tōhoku region") of Japan. It lasted approximately six minutes and caused a [tsunami](/wiki/Tsunami "Tsunami"). It is sometimes known in Japan as the "**Great East Japan Earthquake Disaster**" (東日本大震災, *Higashi Nihon Daishinsai*), among other names.[[en 1]](#cite_note-en_1-30) It is often referred to by its numerical date, **3.11** (read *San ten Ichi-ichi* in Japanese).[[30]](#cite_note-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32)[[32]](#cite_note-33)

The earthquake was the [most powerful ever recorded in Japan](/wiki/List_of_earthquakes_in_Japan "List of earthquakes in Japan"), and the [fourth most powerful recorded in the world](/wiki/Largest_earthquakes_by_magnitude#Strongest_earthquakes_by_magnitude "Largest earthquakes by magnitude") since modern [seismography](/wiki/Seismography "Seismography") began in 1900.[[33]](#cite_note-USGS-34)[[34]](#cite_note-35)[[35]](#cite_note-3news-36) It triggered powerful tsunami waves that may have reached heights of up to 40.5 meters (133 ft) in [Miyako](/wiki/Miyako,_Iwate "Miyako, Iwate") in Tōhoku's [Iwate Prefecture](/wiki/Iwate_Prefecture "Iwate Prefecture"),[[36]](#cite_note-37)[[37]](#cite_note-yomiuri-38) and which, in the [Sendai](/wiki/Sendai "Sendai") area, traveled at 700 km/h (435 mph)[[38]](#cite_note-w-39) and up to 10 km (6 mi) inland.[[39]](#cite_note-roland_buerk-40) Residents of Sendai had only eight to ten minutes of warning, and more than a hundred evacuation sites were washed away.[[38]](#cite_note-w-39) The snowfall which accompanied the tsunami[[40]](#cite_note-GQ_Okawa_school-41) and the freezing temperature hindered rescue works;[[41]](#cite_note-asahi_20110316-42) for instance, in [Ishinomaki](/wiki/Ishinomaki "Ishinomaki"), the city with the most deaths,[[42]](#cite_note-official_deaths_as_of_2021-03-09-43) the temperature was 0 °C (32 °F) as the tsunami hit.[[43]](#cite_note-weather_ishinomaki-44) Official figures released in 2021 reported 19,759 deaths,[[44]](#cite_note-45) 6,242 injured,[[45]](#cite_note-46) 284 firefighters dead from attempts to close preventative fire gates,[[46]](#cite_note-47) and 2,553 people missing[[47]](#cite_note-48). A report in 2015 found that 228,863 people were still living away from their home either temporarily or permanently.[[48]](#cite_note-49)

The tsunami caused the [Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster](/wiki/Fukushima_nuclear_disaster "Fukushima nuclear disaster"), primarily the [meltdowns](/wiki/Nuclear_meltdown "Nuclear meltdown") of three of its reactors, the [discharge of radioactive water in Fukushima](/wiki/Discharge_of_radioactive_water_of_the_Fukushima_Daiichi_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Discharge of radioactive water of the Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Power Plant") and the associated evacuation zones affecting hundreds of thousands of residents.[[49]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-50)[[50]](#cite_note-51) Many electrical generators ran out of fuel. The loss of electrical power halted cooling systems, causing heat to build. Without ventilation, [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") gas accumulated within the upper refueling hall and exploded, forcefully ejecting the refueling hall's blast panels. Residents within a 20 km (12 mi) radius of the Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Power Plant and a 10 km (6.2 mi) radius of the [Fukushima Daini Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Fukushima_Daini_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Fukushima Daini Nuclear Power Plant") were evacuated.

Early estimates placed insured losses from the earthquake alone at US$14.5 to $34.6 billion.[[51]](#cite_note-52) The [Bank of Japan](/wiki/Bank_of_Japan "Bank of Japan") offered [¥](/wiki/Japanese_yen "Japanese yen")15 trillion (US$183 billion) to the banking system on 14 March 2011 in an effort to normalize market conditions.[[52]](#cite_note-53) The estimated economic damage amounted to over $300 billion, making it the [costliest natural disaster in history](/wiki/List_of_disasters_by_cost "List of disasters by cost").[[53]](#cite_note-54)[[54]](#cite_note-55) According to a 2020 study, "the earthquake and its aftermaths resulted in a 0.47 percentage point decline in Japan's real GDP growth in the year following the disaster".[[55]](#cite_note-56)

## Earthquake

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/A_seismogram_of_2011_T%C5%8Dhoku_earthquake_and_tsunami.jpg/250px-A_seismogram_of_2011_T%C5%8Dhoku_earthquake_and_tsunami.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_seismogram_of_2011_T%C5%8Dhoku_earthquake_and_tsunami.jpg)

A [seismogram](/wiki/Seismogram "Seismogram") recorded in [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts"), United States

The [magnitude](/wiki/Moment_magnitude_scale "Moment magnitude scale") 9.1 ([Mw](/wiki/Moment_magnitude_scale "Moment magnitude scale")) [undersea](/wiki/Submarine_earthquake "Submarine earthquake") [megathrust earthquake](/wiki/Megathrust_earthquake "Megathrust earthquake") occurred on 11 March 2011 at 14:46 [JST](/wiki/Japan_Standard_Time "Japan Standard Time") (05:46 UTC) in the north-western Pacific Ocean at a relatively shallow depth of 32 km (20 mi),[[8]](#cite_note-USGS9.0-8)[[56]](#cite_note-57) with its [epicenter](/wiki/Epicenter "Epicenter") approximately 72 km (45 mi) east of the [Oshika Peninsula](/wiki/Oshika_Peninsula "Oshika Peninsula") of [Tōhoku](/wiki/T%C5%8Dhoku_region "Tōhoku region"), Japan, lasting approximately six minutes.[[8]](#cite_note-USGS9.0-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Asahi-ERI-Takashi-9) The earthquake was initially reported as 7.9 Mw by the [USGS](/wiki/USGS "USGS") before it was quickly upgraded to 8.8 Mw, then to 8.9 Mw,[[57]](#cite_note-58) and then finally to 9.0 Mw.[[58]](#cite_note-New_Scientist-59)[[59]](#cite_note-USGS-9.0-Update-60) On 11 July 2016, the USGS further upgraded the earthquake to 9.1. [Sendai](/wiki/Sendai "Sendai") was the nearest major city to the earthquake, 130 km (81 mi) from the epicenter; the earthquake occurred 373 km (232 mi) northeast of [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo").[[8]](#cite_note-USGS9.0-8)

The main earthquake was preceded by a number of large [foreshocks](/wiki/Foreshock "Foreshock"), with hundreds of [aftershocks](/wiki/Aftershock "Aftershock") reported. One of the first major foreshocks was a 7.2 Mw event on 9 March, approximately 40 km (25 mi) from the epicenter of the 11 March earthquake, with another three on the same day in excess of 6.0 Mw.[[8]](#cite_note-USGS9.0-8)[[60]](#cite_note-National_Geographic-61) Following the main earthquake on 11 March, a 7.4 Mw aftershock was reported at 15:08 JST (6:06 UTC), succeeded by a 7.9 Mw at 15:15 JST (6:16 UTC) and a 7.7 Mw at 15:26 JST (6:26 UTC).[[61]](#cite_note-62) Over 800 aftershocks of magnitude 4.5 Mw or greater occurred after the initial quake.[[62]](#cite_note-63) Aftershocks followed [Omori's law](/wiki/Aftershock#Omori's_law "Aftershock"), which states that the rate of aftershocks declines with the reciprocal of the time since the main quake. The aftershocks thus tapered off over time, but continued for years after the initial quake,[[63]](#cite_note-CBSMcNutt-64) including one on 26 October 2013 (local time) of magnitude 7.1 Mw.[[64]](#cite_note-65)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/9/96/C0001xgp_wcmt_smt.png)](/wiki/File:C0001xgp_wcmt_smt.png)

A visual depiction of the [type of faulting](/wiki/Focal_mechanism "Focal mechanism") that caused the main tremor, low-angle [thrusting](/wiki/Thrust_fault "Thrust fault") on a west-dipping [fault plane](/wiki/Fault_(geology) "Fault (geology)")

The earthquake moved [Honshu](/wiki/Honshu "Honshu") 2.4 meters (7 ft 10 in) east, shifted the Earth on its axis by estimates of between 10 and 25 cm (4 and 10 in),[[65]](#cite_note-DW-66)[[66]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Chang2011-03-13-67)[[67]](#cite_note-Chai-68) increased Earth's rotational speed by 1.8 [μs](/wiki/%CE%9Cs "Μs") per day,[[68]](#cite_note-69) and generated [infrasound](/wiki/Infrasound "Infrasound") waves detected in perturbations of the low-orbiting [Gravity Field and Steady-State Ocean Circulation Explorer](/wiki/Gravity_Field_and_Steady-State_Ocean_Circulation_Explorer "Gravity Field and Steady-State Ocean Circulation Explorer") satellite.[[69]](#cite_note-esa.int-70)
Initially, the earthquake caused part of Honshu's Pacific coast to sink by roughly a meter, but after about three years, the coast rose back and then kept on rising to exceed its original height.[[70]](#cite_note-71)[[71]](#cite_note-72)[[72]](#cite_note-73)[[73]](#cite_note-74)

### Geology

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/2011_Tohoku_earthquake_mechanism_main_en.png/250px-2011_Tohoku_earthquake_mechanism_main_en.png)](/wiki/File:2011_Tohoku_earthquake_mechanism_main_en.png)

Mechanism of 2011 Tōhoku earthquake

This megathrust earthquake was a recurrence of the mechanism of the earlier [869 Sanriku earthquake](/wiki/869_Sanriku_earthquake "869 Sanriku earthquake"), which has been estimated as having a magnitude of at least 8.4 Mw, which also created a large tsunami that inundated the Sendai plain.[[38]](#cite_note-w-39)[[74]](#cite_note-75)[[75]](#cite_note-76) Three tsunami deposits have been identified within the [Holocene](/wiki/Holocene "Holocene") sequence of the plain, all formed within the last 3,000 years, suggesting an 800 to 1,100 year recurrence interval for large tsunamigenic earthquakes. In 2001 it was reckoned that there was a high likelihood of a large tsunami hitting the Sendai plain as more than 1,100 years had then elapsed.[[76]](#cite_note-Minoura-77) In 2007, the probability of an earthquake with a magnitude of Mw 8.1–8.3 was estimated as 99% within the following 30 years.[[77]](#cite_note-Satake-78)

This earthquake occurred where the [Pacific plate](/wiki/Pacific_plate "Pacific plate") is [subducting](/wiki/Subduction "Subduction") under the plate beneath northern Honshu.[[66]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Chang2011-03-13-67)[[78]](#cite_note-grauniad-79) The Pacific plate, which moves at a rate of 8 to 9 cm (3.1 to 3.5 in) per year, dips under Honshu's underlying plate, building large amounts of [elastic energy](/wiki/Elastic_energy "Elastic energy"). This motion pushes the upper plate down until the accumulated stress causes a seismic slip-rupture event. The break caused the sea floor to rise by several meters.[[78]](#cite_note-grauniad-79) The magnitude of this earthquake was a surprise to some seismologists.[[79]](#cite_note-:2-80) A quake of this magnitude usually has a rupture length of at least 500 km (310 mi) and generally requires a long, relatively straight fault surface. Because the plate boundary and [subduction](/wiki/Subduction "Subduction") zone in the area of the Honshu rupture is not very straight, it is unusual for the magnitude of its earthquake to exceed 8.5 Mw.[[79]](#cite_note-:2-80) The [hypocentral region](/wiki/Hypocenter "Hypocenter") of this earthquake extended from offshore [Iwate Prefecture](/wiki/Iwate_Prefecture "Iwate Prefecture") to offshore [Ibaraki Prefecture](/wiki/Ibaraki_Prefecture "Ibaraki Prefecture").[[80]](#cite_note-81) The [Japan Meteorological Agency](/wiki/Japan_Meteorological_Agency "Japan Meteorological Agency") said that the earthquake may have ruptured the fault zone from Iwate to Ibaraki with a length of 500 km (310 mi) and a width of 200 km (120 mi).[[81]](#cite_note-82)[[82]](#cite_note-83) Analysis showed that this earthquake consisted of a set of three events.[[83]](#cite_note-84) Other major earthquakes with tsunamis struck the [Sanriku Coast](/wiki/Sanriku_Coast "Sanriku Coast") region [in 1896](/wiki/1896_Sanriku_earthquake "1896 Sanriku earthquake") and [in 1933](/wiki/1933_Sanriku_earthquake "1933 Sanriku earthquake").

The source area of this earthquake has a relatively high coupling coefficient surrounded by areas of relatively low coupling coefficients in the west, north, and south. From the averaged coupling coefficient of 0.5–0.8 in the source area and the seismic moment, it was estimated that the slip deficit of this earthquake was accumulated over a period of 260–880 years, which is consistent with the recurrence interval of such great earthquakes estimated from the tsunami deposit data. The seismic moment of this earthquake accounts for about 93% of the estimated cumulative moment from 1926 to March 2011. Hence, earthquakes in this area with magnitudes of about 7 since 1926 had only released part of the accumulated energy. In the area near the trench, the coupling coefficient is high, which could act as the source of the large tsunami.[[84]](#cite_note-85)

Most of the foreshocks are interplate earthquakes with thrust-type focal mechanisms. Both interplate and intraplate earthquakes appeared in the aftershocks offshore Sanriku coast with considerable proportions.[[85]](#cite_note-86)

### Energy

The [surface energy](/wiki/Moment_magnitude_scale#Radiated_seismic_energy "Moment magnitude scale") of the seismic waves from the earthquake was calculated to be 1.9×1017 [joules](/wiki/Joule "Joule"),[[86]](#cite_note-87) which is nearly double that of the 9.1 Mw [2004 Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami](/wiki/2004_Indian_Ocean_earthquake_and_tsunami "2004 Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami") that killed 230,000 people. If harnessed, the seismic energy from this earthquake would power a city the size of Los Angeles for an entire year.[[63]](#cite_note-CBSMcNutt-64) The [seismic moment](/wiki/Seismic_moment "Seismic moment") (M0), which represents a physical size for the event, was calculated by the USGS at 3.9×1022 joules,[[87]](#cite_note-88) slightly less than the 2004 Indian Ocean quake.

### Ground motion

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Shindomap_2011-03-11_Tohoku_earthquake.png/250px-Shindomap_2011-03-11_Tohoku_earthquake.png)](/wiki/File:Shindomap_2011-03-11_Tohoku_earthquake.png)

[Seismic intensity](/wiki/Japan_Meteorological_Agency_seismic_intensity_scale "Japan Meteorological Agency seismic intensity scale") observations resulting from mainshock

The strong ground motion registered at the maximum of *Shindo* 7 on the [Japan Meteorological Agency seismic intensity scale](/wiki/Japan_Meteorological_Agency_seismic_intensity_scale "Japan Meteorological Agency seismic intensity scale") in [Kurihara](/wiki/Kurihara,_Miyagi "Kurihara, Miyagi"), [Miyagi Prefecture](/wiki/Miyagi_Prefecture "Miyagi Prefecture").[[88]](#cite_note-jma-89) Three other prefectures—[Fukushima](/wiki/Fukushima_Prefecture "Fukushima Prefecture"), [Ibaraki](/wiki/Ibaraki_Prefecture "Ibaraki Prefecture") and [Tochigi](/wiki/Tochigi_Prefecture "Tochigi Prefecture")—recorded a 6 upper on the JMA scale. Seismic stations in [Iwate](/wiki/Iwate_Prefecture "Iwate Prefecture"), [Gunma](/wiki/Gunma_Prefecture "Gunma Prefecture"), [Saitama](/wiki/Saitama_Prefecture "Saitama Prefecture") and [Chiba Prefectures](/wiki/Chiba_Prefecture "Chiba Prefecture") measured a 6 lower, while [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo") and five other prefectures recorded 5 upper.

Japan's National Research Institute for Earth Science and Disaster Prevention (NIED) calculated a [peak ground acceleration](/wiki/Peak_ground_acceleration "Peak ground acceleration") of 2.99 [*g*](/wiki/Peak_ground_acceleration "Peak ground acceleration") (29.33 m/s2).[[89]](#cite_note-90)[[en 2]](#cite_note-en_2-93) A USGS seismic installation at Kurihara recorded 2.751 *g* in ground acceleration (pga) and 98.21 cm/s (38.67 in/s) in ground velocity (pgv); the station data corresponded to a [Modified Mercalli intensity](/wiki/Modified_Mercalli_intensity "Modified Mercalli intensity") (MMI) of IX (*Violent*), which was also recorded at five other installations at [Ōsaki](/wiki/%C5%8Csaki,_Miyagi "Ōsaki, Miyagi"), [Onagawa](/wiki/Onagawa,_Miyagi "Onagawa, Miyagi") and [Iwanuma](/wiki/Iwanuma "Iwanuma"), Miyagi, [Haga, Tochigi](/wiki/Haga,_Tochigi "Haga, Tochigi"), and the [Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Fukushima_Daiichi_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Power Plant"); tremors exceeding MMI VI (*Strong*) or higher were felt in the [Greater Tokyo Area](/wiki/Greater_Tokyo_Area "Greater Tokyo Area"), with a seismic station in [Kōtō](/wiki/K%C5%8Dt%C5%8D "Kōtō") recording a pga of 0.2552 *g* and a pgv of 43.96 cm/s (17.31 in/s), corresponding to MMI VIII (*Severe*) shaking.[[3]](#cite_note-usgspga-3) Tremors from the earthquake were also felt in the [Northern Mariana Islands](/wiki/Northern_Mariana_Islands "Northern Mariana Islands"), the [Korean Peninsula](/wiki/Korean_Peninsula "Korean Peninsula"), [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), [Northeast China](/wiki/Northeast_China "Northeast China") and the [Russian Far East](/wiki/Russian_Far_East "Russian Far East").[[8]](#cite_note-USGS9.0-8)

### Geophysical effects

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/SeismicAreas_JapanTrenchEarthquakes.png/250px-SeismicAreas_JapanTrenchEarthquakes.png)](/wiki/File:SeismicAreas_JapanTrenchEarthquakes.png)

Hypocentral region boundaries (source: the Japanese [Headquarters for Earthquake Research Promotion](/wiki/Headquarters_for_Earthquake_Research_Promotion "Headquarters for Earthquake Research Promotion"))

Portions of northeastern Japan shifted by as much as 2.4 meters (7 ft 10 in) closer to North America,[[65]](#cite_note-DW-66)[[66]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Chang2011-03-13-67) making some sections of Japan's landmass wider than before.[[66]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Chang2011-03-13-67) Those areas of Japan closest to the epicenter experienced the largest shifts.[[66]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Chang2011-03-13-67) A 400-kilometer (250 mi) stretch of coastline dropped vertically by 0.6 meters (2 ft 0 in), allowing the tsunami to travel farther and faster onto land.[[66]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Chang2011-03-13-67) One early estimate suggested that the Pacific plate may have moved westward by up to 20 meters (66 ft),[[93]](#cite_note-95) and another early estimate put the amount of slippage at as much as 40 m (130 ft).[[94]](#cite_note-96) On 6 April, the Japanese coast guard said that the quake shifted the seabed near the epicenter 24 meters (79 ft) and elevated the seabed off the coast of Miyagi Prefecture by 3 meters (9.8 ft).[[95]](#cite_note-97) A report by the [Japan Agency for Marine-Earth Science and Technology](/wiki/Japan_Agency_for_Marine-Earth_Science_and_Technology "Japan Agency for Marine-Earth Science and Technology"), published in *[Science](/wiki/Science_(journal) "Science (journal)")* on 2 December 2011, concluded that the seabed in the area between the epicenter and the [Japan Trench](/wiki/Japan_Trench "Japan Trench") moved 50 meters (160 ft) east-southeast and rose about 7 meters (23 ft) as a result of the quake. The report also stated that the quake had caused several major landslides on the seabed in the affected area.[[96]](#cite_note-98)

The Earth's [axis](/wiki/Rotation_around_a_fixed_axis "Rotation around a fixed axis") shifted by estimates of between 10 and 25 cm (4 and 10 in).[[65]](#cite_note-DW-66)[[66]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Chang2011-03-13-67)[[67]](#cite_note-Chai-68) This deviation led to a number of small planetary changes, including the [length of a day](/wiki/Earth%27s_rotation "Earth's rotation"), the [tilt of the Earth](/wiki/Axial_tilt#Earth "Axial tilt"), and the [Chandler wobble](/wiki/Chandler_wobble "Chandler wobble").[[67]](#cite_note-Chai-68) The speed of the Earth's rotation increased, shortening the day by 1.8 [microseconds](/wiki/Microsecond "Microsecond") due to the redistribution of Earth's mass.[[97]](#cite_note-day_length-99) The axial shift was caused by the redistribution of mass on the Earth's surface, which changed the planet's [moment of inertia](/wiki/Moment_of_inertia "Moment of inertia"). Because of [conservation of angular momentum](/wiki/Conservation_of_angular_momentum "Conservation of angular momentum"), such changes of [inertia](/wiki/Inertia "Inertia") result in small changes to the Earth's rate of rotation.[[98]](#cite_note-100) These are expected changes[[67]](#cite_note-Chai-68) for an earthquake of this magnitude.[[65]](#cite_note-DW-66)[[97]](#cite_note-day_length-99) The earthquake also generated infrasound waves detected by perturbations in the orbit of the [GOCE](/wiki/GOCE "GOCE") satellite, which thus [serendipitously](/wiki/Role_of_chance_in_scientific_discoveries "Role of chance in scientific discoveries") became the first seismograph in orbit.[[69]](#cite_note-esa.int-70)

Following the earthquake, cracks were observed to have formed in the roof of [Mount Fuji](/wiki/Mount_Fuji "Mount Fuji")'s [magma chamber](/wiki/Magma_chamber "Magma chamber").[[99]](#cite_note-101)

[Seiches](/wiki/Seiche "Seiche") observed in [Sognefjorden](/wiki/Sognefjord "Sognefjord"), Norway, were attributed to distant [S waves](/wiki/S_wave "S wave") and [Love waves](/wiki/Love_wave "Love wave") generated by the earthquake. These seiches began to occur roughly half an hour after the main shock hit Japan, and continued to occur for 3 hours, during which waves of up to 1.5 meters high were observed.[[100]](#cite_note-102)

[Soil liquefaction](/wiki/Soil_liquefaction "Soil liquefaction") was evident in areas of [reclaimed land](/wiki/Reclaimed_land "Reclaimed land") around Tokyo, particularly in [Urayasu](/wiki/Urayasu,_Chiba "Urayasu, Chiba"),[[101]](#cite_note-103)[[102]](#cite_note-104) [Chiba City](/wiki/Chiba,_Chiba "Chiba, Chiba"), [Funabashi](/wiki/Funabashi,_Chiba "Funabashi, Chiba"), [Narashino](/wiki/Narashino,_Chiba "Narashino, Chiba") (all in [Chiba Prefecture](/wiki/Chiba_Prefecture "Chiba Prefecture")) and in the [Koto](/wiki/Koto,_Tokyo "Koto, Tokyo"), [Edogawa](/wiki/Edogawa,_Tokyo "Edogawa, Tokyo"), [Minato](/wiki/Minato,_Tokyo "Minato, Tokyo"), [Chūō](/wiki/Ch%C5%AB%C5%8D,_Tokyo "Chūō, Tokyo"), and [Ōta](/wiki/%C5%8Cta,_Tokyo "Ōta, Tokyo") wards of Tokyo. Approximately 30 homes or buildings were destroyed and 1,046 other buildings were damaged to varying degrees.[[103]](#cite_note-105) Nearby [Haneda Airport](/wiki/Haneda_Airport "Haneda Airport"), built mostly on reclaimed land, was not damaged. [Odaiba](/wiki/Odaiba "Odaiba") also experienced liquefaction, but damage was minimal.[[104]](#cite_note-106)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Soil-liquefaction_at_Shinkiba_after_2011_Tohoku_Pacific_Ocean_offshore_earthquake.jpg/250px-Soil-liquefaction_at_Shinkiba_after_2011_Tohoku_Pacific_Ocean_offshore_earthquake.jpg)](/wiki/File:Soil-liquefaction_at_Shinkiba_after_2011_Tohoku_Pacific_Ocean_offshore_earthquake.jpg)

[Soil liquefaction](/wiki/Soil_liquefaction "Soil liquefaction") in [Kōtō](/wiki/K%C5%8Dt%C5%8D "Kōtō"), Tokyo

[Shinmoedake](/wiki/Shinmoedake "Shinmoedake"), a volcano in [Kyushu](/wiki/Kyushu "Kyushu"), erupted three days after the earthquake. The volcano had previously erupted in January 2011; it is not known if the later eruption was linked to the earthquake.[[105]](#cite_note-107) In [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica"), the seismic waves from the earthquake were reported to have caused the [Whillans Ice Stream](/wiki/Whillans_Ice_Stream "Whillans Ice Stream") to slip by about 0.5 meters (1 ft 8 in).[[106]](#cite_note-108)

The first sign international researchers had that the earthquake caused such a dramatic change in the Earth's rotation came from the United States Geological Survey which monitors Global Positioning Satellite (GPS) stations across the world. The Survey team had several GPS monitors located near the scene of the earthquake. The GPS station located nearest the epicenter moved almost 4 m (13 ft). This motivated government researchers to look into other ways the earthquake may have had large scale effects on the planet. Calculations at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory determined that the Earth's rotation was changed by the earthquake to the point where the days are now 1.8 [microseconds](/wiki/Microsecond "Microsecond") shorter.[[107]](#cite_note-109)

### Aftershocks

Further information: [List of foreshocks and aftershocks of the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake](/wiki/List_of_foreshocks_and_aftershocks_of_the_2011_T%C5%8Dhoku_earthquake "List of foreshocks and aftershocks of the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Map_of_Sendai_Earthquake_2011.jpg/250px-Map_of_Sendai_Earthquake_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Sendai_Earthquake_2011.jpg)

Tōhoku earthquake and aftershocks from 11 to 14 March

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/2011_Earthquake_and_Tsunami_near_Sendai%2C_Japan.jpg/250px-2011_Earthquake_and_Tsunami_near_Sendai%2C_Japan.jpg)](/wiki/File:2011_Earthquake_and_Tsunami_near_Sendai,_Japan.jpg)

Satellite image shows snowfall on agricultural fields in Sendai area. See [§ Weather conditions](#Weather_conditions) paragraph for details.

Japan experienced over 1,000 aftershocks since the earthquake, with 80 registering over magnitude 6.0 Mw and several of which have been over magnitude 7.0 Mw.

A magnitude 7.4 Mw at 15:08 (JST), 7.9 Mw at 15:15 and a 7.7 Mw quake at 15:26 all occurred on 11 March.[[108]](#cite_note-110)

A month later, a [major aftershock](/wiki/April_2011_Miyagi_earthquake "April 2011 Miyagi earthquake") struck offshore on 7 April with a magnitude of 7.1 Mw. Its epicenter was underwater, 66 km (41 mi) off the coast of Sendai. The [Japan Meteorological Agency](/wiki/Japan_Meteorological_Agency "Japan Meteorological Agency") assigned a magnitude of 7.4 MJMA, while the [United States Geological Survey](/wiki/United_States_Geological_Survey "United States Geological Survey") lowered it to 7.1 Mw.[[109]](#cite_note-111) At least four people were killed, and electricity was cut off across much of northern Japan including the loss of external power to [Higashidōri Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Higashid%C5%8Dri_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Higashidōri Nuclear Power Plant") and [Rokkasho Reprocessing Plant](/wiki/Rokkasho_Reprocessing_Plant "Rokkasho Reprocessing Plant").[[110]](#cite_note-112)[[111]](#cite_note-113)[[112]](#cite_note-114)

Four days later on 11 April, another [magnitude 7.1 Mw aftershock](/wiki/April_2011_Fukushima_earthquake "April 2011 Fukushima earthquake") struck Fukushima, causing additional damage and killing a total of three people.[[113]](#cite_note-115)[[114]](#cite_note-116)

On 7 December 2012 a [large aftershock of magnitude 7.3 Mw](/wiki/2012_Kamaishi_earthquake "2012 Kamaishi earthquake") caused a minor tsunami, and again on 26 October 2013 a small tsunami was recorded after a 7.1 Mw aftershock.[[115]](#cite_note-117)

As of 16 March 2012 aftershocks continued, totaling 1887 events over magnitude 4.0; a regularly updated map showing all shocks of magnitude 4.5 and above near or off the east coast of Honshu in the last seven days[[116]](#cite_note-118) showed over 20 events.[[117]](#cite_note-119)

As of 11 March 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=2011_T%C5%8Dhoku_earthquake_and_tsunami&action=edit) there had been 869 aftershocks of 5.0 Mw or greater, 118 of 6.0 Mw or greater, and 9 over 7.0 Mw as reported by the Japan Meteorological Agency.[[118]](#cite_note-120)

The number of aftershocks was associated with decreased health across Japan.[[119]](#cite_note-121)

On 13 February 2021, a [magnitude 7.1–7.3 earthquake](/wiki/2021_Fukushima_earthquake "2021 Fukushima earthquake") struck off the coast of Sendai. It caused some damage in Miyagi and Fukushima prefectures. One person was killed, and 185 were injured.[[120]](#cite_note-:1-122)[[121]](#cite_note-123)

### Land subsidence

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Subsidence_in_Shin-Urayasu_Sta_after_2011_Sendai_earthquake.JPG/250px-Subsidence_in_Shin-Urayasu_Sta_after_2011_Sendai_earthquake.JPG)](/wiki/File:Subsidence_in_Shin-Urayasu_Sta_after_2011_Sendai_earthquake.JPG)

[Land subsidence](/wiki/Land_subsidence "Land subsidence") and [soil liquefaction](/wiki/Soil_liquefaction "Soil liquefaction") at [Shin-Urayasu Station](/wiki/Shin-Urayasu_Station "Shin-Urayasu Station") in [Urayasu, Chiba](/wiki/Urayasu,_Chiba "Urayasu, Chiba"), near [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo")

The [Geospatial Information Authority of Japan](/wiki/Geospatial_Information_Authority_of_Japan "Geospatial Information Authority of Japan") reported land [subsidence](/wiki/Subsidence "Subsidence") based on the height of [triangulation stations](/wiki/Triangulation_station "Triangulation station") in the area measured by [GPS](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System") as compared to their previous values from 14 April 2011.[[122]](#cite_note-124)

* [Miyako, Iwate](/wiki/Miyako,_Iwate "Miyako, Iwate") – 0.50 meters (1 ft 8 in)
* [Yamada, Iwate](/wiki/Yamada,_Iwate "Yamada, Iwate") – 0.53 meters (1 ft 9 in)
* [Ōtsuchi, Iwate](/wiki/%C5%8Ctsuchi,_Iwate "Ōtsuchi, Iwate") – 0.35 meters (1 ft 2 in)[[123]](#cite_note-yumi20110415-p33-125)
* [Kamaishi, Iwate](/wiki/Kamaishi,_Iwate "Kamaishi, Iwate") – 0.66 meters (2 ft 2 in)
* [Ōfunato, Iwate](/wiki/%C5%8Cfunato,_Iwate "Ōfunato, Iwate") – 0.73 meters (2 ft 5 in)
* [Rikuzentakata, Iwate](/wiki/Rikuzentakata,_Iwate "Rikuzentakata, Iwate") – 0.84 meters (2 ft 9 in)
* [Kesennuma, Miyagi](/wiki/Kesennuma,_Miyagi "Kesennuma, Miyagi") – 0.74 meters (2 ft 5 in)
* [Minamisanriku, Miyagi](/wiki/Minamisanriku,_Miyagi "Minamisanriku, Miyagi") – 0.69 meters (2 ft 3 in)
* [Oshika Peninsula](/wiki/Oshika_Peninsula "Oshika Peninsula"), [Miyagi](/wiki/Miyagi_Prefecture "Miyagi Prefecture") – 1.2 meters (3 ft 11 in)[[123]](#cite_note-yumi20110415-p33-125)
* [Ishinomaki, Miyagi](/wiki/Ishinomaki,_Miyagi "Ishinomaki, Miyagi") – 0.78 meters (2 ft 7 in)
* [Higashimatsushima, Miyagi](/wiki/Higashimatsushima,_Miyagi "Higashimatsushima, Miyagi") – 0.43 meters (1 ft 5 in)
* [Iwanuma, Miyagi](/wiki/Iwanuma,_Miyagi "Iwanuma, Miyagi") – 0.47 meters (1 ft 7 in)
* [Sōma, Fukushima](/wiki/S%C5%8Dma,_Fukushima "Sōma, Fukushima") – 0.29 meters (11 in)

Scientists say that the subsidence is permanent. As a result, the communities in question are now more susceptible to flooding during high tides.[[124]](#cite_note-126)

### Earthquake early warning system

One minute before the earthquake was felt in Tokyo, the [Earthquake Early Warning](/wiki/Earthquake_Early_Warning_(Japan) "Earthquake Early Warning (Japan)") system, which includes more than 1,000 [seismometers](/wiki/Seismometer "Seismometer") in Japan, sent out warnings of impending strong shaking to millions. It is believed that the early warning by the [Japan Meteorological Agency](/wiki/Japan_Meteorological_Agency "Japan Meteorological Agency") (JMA) saved many lives.[[125]](#cite_note-127)[[126]](#cite_note-128) The warning for the general public was delivered about eight seconds after the first [P wave](/wiki/P_wave "P wave") was detected, or about 31 seconds after the earthquake occurred. However, the estimated intensities were smaller than the actual ones in some places, especially in Kanto, [Koshinetsu](/wiki/Koshinetsu "Koshinetsu"), and Northern Tōhoku regions where the populace warning did not trigger. According to the [Japan Meteorological Agency](/wiki/Japan_Meteorological_Agency "Japan Meteorological Agency"), reasons for the underestimation include a saturated magnitude scale when using maximum amplitude as input, failure to fully take into account the area of the [hypocenter](/wiki/Hypocenter "Hypocenter"), and the initial amplitude of the earthquake being less than that which would be predicted by an empirical relationship.[[127]](#cite_note-129)[[128]](#cite_note-130)[[129]](#cite_note-zisin.64.155-131)[[130]](#cite_note-132)

There were also cases where large differences between estimated intensities by the Earthquake Early Warning system and the actual intensities occurred in the aftershocks and triggered earthquakes. Such discrepancies in the warning were attributed by the JMA to the system's inability to distinguish between two different earthquakes that happened at around same time, as well as to the reduced number of reporting seismometers due to power outages and connection failures.[[131]](#cite_note-133) The system's software was subsequently modified to handle this kind of situation.[[132]](#cite_note-134)

## Tsunami

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/2011Sendai-NOAA-Energylhvpd9-05.jpg/250px-2011Sendai-NOAA-Energylhvpd9-05.jpg)](/wiki/File:2011Sendai-NOAA-Energylhvpd9-05.jpg)

[NOAA](/wiki/National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration "National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration") tsunami energy map

An upthrust of 6 to 8 meters (20 to 26 ft) along a 180-kilometer (110 mi)-wide seabed at 60 kilometers (37 mi) offshore from the east coast of Tōhoku[[133]](#cite_note-135) resulted in a major [tsunami](/wiki/Tsunami "Tsunami") that brought destruction along the [Pacific](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean") coastline of Japan's northern islands. Thousands of people died and entire towns were devastated. The tsunami propagated throughout the Pacific Ocean region reaching the entire Pacific coast of North and South America from [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") to [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile"). Warnings were issued and evacuations were carried out in many countries bordering the Pacific. Although the tsunami affected many of these places, the heights of the waves were minor.[[134]](#cite_note-a2-136)[[135]](#cite_note-a3-137)[[136]](#cite_note-a4-138) Chile's Pacific coast, one of the farthest from Japan at about 17,000 kilometers (11,000 mi) away, was struck by waves 2 meters (6.6 ft) high,[[137]](#cite_note-139)[[138]](#cite_note-140)[[139]](#cite_note-141) compared with an estimated wave height of 38.9 meters (128 ft) at Omoe peninsula, Miyako city, Japan.[[37]](#cite_note-yomiuri-38)

### Japan

The [tsunami warning](/wiki/Tsunami_warning_system "Tsunami warning system") issued by the [Japan Meteorological Agency](/wiki/Japan_Meteorological_Agency "Japan Meteorological Agency") was the most serious on its warning scale; it was rated as a "major tsunami", being at least 3 meters (9.8 ft) high.[[140]](#cite_note-142) The actual height prediction varied, the greatest being for Miyagi at 6 meters (20 ft) high.[[141]](#cite_note-143) The tsunami inundated a total area of approximately 561 square kilometers (217 sq mi) in Japan.[[142]](#cite_note-144)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/2011-Sendai-Tsunami-DART-21413-AB.png/250px-2011-Sendai-Tsunami-DART-21413-AB.png)](/wiki/File:2011-Sendai-Tsunami-DART-21413-AB.png)

Water column height on 11 March 2011 at [DART](/wiki/Deep-ocean_Assessment_and_Reporting_of_Tsunamis "Deep-ocean Assessment and Reporting of Tsunamis") Station, 690 NM southeast of Tokyo

The earthquake took place at 14:46 JST (UTC 05:46) around 67 kilometers (42 mi) from the nearest point on Japan's coastline, and initial estimates indicated the tsunami would have taken 10 to 30 minutes to reach the areas first affected, and then areas farther north and south based on the geography of the coastline.[[143]](#cite_note-145)[[144]](#cite_note-146) At 15:55 JST, a tsunami was observed flooding [Sendai Airport](/wiki/Sendai_Airport "Sendai Airport"), which is located near the coast of [Miyagi Prefecture](/wiki/Miyagi_Prefecture "Miyagi Prefecture"),[[145]](#cite_note-AVH1-147)[[146]](#cite_note-10m-148) with waves sweeping away cars and planes and flooding various buildings as they traveled inland.[[147]](#cite_note-nhk-english-stream-149)[[148]](#cite_note-heraldsun1-150) The impact of the tsunami in and around Sendai Airport was filmed by an [NHK](/wiki/NHK "NHK") News helicopter, showing a number of vehicles on local roads trying to escape the approaching wave and being engulfed by it.[[149]](#cite_note-151) A 4-meter-high (13 ft) tsunami hit [Iwate Prefecture](/wiki/Iwate_Prefecture "Iwate Prefecture").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Wakabayashi Ward](/wiki/Wakabayashi-ku,_Sendai "Wakabayashi-ku, Sendai") in Sendai was also particularly hard hit.[[150]](#cite_note-152) At least 101 designated tsunami evacuation sites were hit by the wave.[[38]](#cite_note-w-39)[[151]](#cite_note-153)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Devastation_after_tsunami_in_Rikuzentakata.jpg/250px-Devastation_after_tsunami_in_Rikuzentakata.jpg)](/wiki/File:Devastation_after_tsunami_in_Rikuzentakata.jpg)

The city of [Rikuzentakata](/wiki/Rikuzentakata,_Iwate "Rikuzentakata, Iwate"), [Iwate Prefecture](/wiki/Iwate_Prefecture "Iwate Prefecture"), suffered extensive damage from the tsunami, with almost the entirety of the lower area of the city being destroyed.

Like the [2004 Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami](/wiki/2004_Indian_Ocean_earthquake_and_tsunami "2004 Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami"), the damage by surging water, though much more localized, was far more deadly and destructive than the actual quake. Entire towns were destroyed in tsunami-hit areas in Japan, including 9,500 missing in [Minamisanriku](/wiki/Minamisanriku,_Miyagi "Minamisanriku, Miyagi");[[152]](#cite_note-154) one thousand bodies had been recovered in the town by 14 March 2011.[[153]](#cite_note-155)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Fudai_Floodgate_%28sea_side%29.JPG/250px-Fudai_Floodgate_%28sea_side%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Fudai_Floodgate_(sea_side).JPG)

While the tsunami was able to surmount [sea walls](/wiki/Sea_wall "Sea wall") and [floodgates](/wiki/Floodgate "Floodgate") in many areas along the coastline of the Tohoku region, [Fudai, Iwate](/wiki/Fudai,_Iwate "Fudai, Iwate"), was spared destruction due to its 15.5-meter (51 ft) floodgate, which successfully withstood the waves.[[154]](#cite_note-156)

Among the factors in the high death toll was the unexpectedly large water surge. The [sea walls](/wiki/Sea_wall "Sea wall") in several cities had been built to protect against tsunamis of much lower heights. Also, many people caught in the tsunami thought they were on high enough ground to be safe.[[155]](#cite_note-157) According to a special committee on disaster prevention designated by the Japanese government, the tsunami protection policy had been intended to deal with only tsunamis that had been scientifically proved to occur repeatedly. The committee advised that future policy should be to protect against the highest possible tsunami. Because tsunami walls had been [overtopped](/wiki/Wave_overtopping "Wave overtopping"), the committee also suggested, besides building taller tsunami walls, also teaching citizens how to evacuate if a large-scale tsunami should strike.[[156]](#cite_note-158)[[157]](#cite_note-159)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/SendaiAirportMarch16.jpg/250px-SendaiAirportMarch16.jpg)](/wiki/File:SendaiAirportMarch16.jpg)

Tsunami flooding on the [Sendai Airport](/wiki/Sendai_Airport "Sendai Airport") runway

Large parts of [Kuji](/wiki/Kuji,_Iwate "Kuji, Iwate") and the southern section of [Ōfunato](/wiki/%C5%8Cfunato "Ōfunato") including the port area were almost entirely destroyed.[[158]](#cite_note-160)[[159]](#cite_note-161) Also largely destroyed was [Rikuzentakata](/wiki/Rikuzentakata,_Iwate "Rikuzentakata, Iwate"), where the tsunami was three stories high.[[160]](#cite_note-162)[[161]](#cite_note-163) Other cities destroyed or heavily damaged by the tsunami include [Kamaishi](/wiki/Kamaishi,_Iwate "Kamaishi, Iwate"), [Miyako](/wiki/Miyako,_Iwate "Miyako, Iwate"), [Ōtsuchi](/wiki/%C5%8Ctsuchi,_Iwate "Ōtsuchi, Iwate"), and [Yamada](/wiki/Yamada,_Iwate "Yamada, Iwate") (in Iwate Prefecture), [Namie](/wiki/Namie,_Fukushima "Namie, Fukushima"), [Sōma](/wiki/S%C5%8Dma,_Fukushima "Sōma, Fukushima"), and [Minamisōma](/wiki/Minamis%C5%8Dma,_Fukushima "Minamisōma, Fukushima") (in Fukushima Prefecture) and [Shichigahama](/wiki/Shichigahama,_Miyagi "Shichigahama, Miyagi"), [Higashimatsushima](/wiki/Higashimatsushima,_Miyagi "Higashimatsushima, Miyagi"), [Onagawa](/wiki/Onagawa,_Miyagi "Onagawa, Miyagi"), [Natori](/wiki/Natori,_Miyagi "Natori, Miyagi"), [Ishinomaki](/wiki/Ishinomaki,_Miyagi "Ishinomaki, Miyagi"), and [Kesennuma](/wiki/Kesennuma,_Miyagi "Kesennuma, Miyagi") (in Miyagi Prefecture).[[162]](#cite_note-164)[[163]](#cite_note-165)[[164]](#cite_note-166)[[165]](#cite_note-167)[[166]](#cite_note-168)[[167]](#cite_note-169)[[168]](#cite_note-170) The most severe effects of the tsunami were felt along a 670-kilometer-long (420 mi) stretch of coastline from [Erimo, Hokkaido](/wiki/Erimo,_Hokkaido "Erimo, Hokkaido"), in the north to [Ōarai, Ibaraki](/wiki/%C5%8Carai,_Ibaraki "Ōarai, Ibaraki"), in the south, with most of the destruction in that area occurring in the hour following the earthquake.[[169]](#cite_note-171) Near Ōarai, people captured images of a huge [whirlpool](/wiki/Whirlpool "Whirlpool") that had been generated by the tsunami.[[170]](#cite_note-172) The tsunami washed away the sole bridge to Miyatojima, Miyagi, isolating the island's 900 residents.[[171]](#cite_note-173) A 2 meters (6 ft 7 in) high tsunami hit [Chiba Prefecture](/wiki/Chiba_Prefecture "Chiba Prefecture") about 2+1⁄2 hours after the quake, causing heavy damage to cities such as [Asahi](/wiki/Asahi,_Chiba "Asahi, Chiba").[[172]](#cite_note-174)

On 13 March 2011, the [Japan Meteorological Agency](/wiki/Japan_Meteorological_Agency "Japan Meteorological Agency") (JMA) published details of tsunami observations recorded around the coastline of Japan following the earthquake. These observations included tsunami maximum readings of over 3 meters (9.8 ft) at the following locations and times on 11 March 2011, following the earthquake at 14:46 JST:[[173]](#cite_note-175)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Tsunami_map_Tohoku2011.svg/250px-Tsunami_map_Tohoku2011.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Tsunami_map_Tohoku2011.svg)

Peak tsunami wave height summits, color-coded with red representing most severe

* 15:12 JST – off [Kamaishi](/wiki/Kamaishi,_Iwate "Kamaishi, Iwate") – 6.8 meters (22 ft)
* 15:15 JST – [Ōfunato](/wiki/%C5%8Cfunato "Ōfunato") – 3.2 meters (10 ft) or higher
* 15:20 JST – [Ishinomaki-shi Ayukawa](/wiki/Ishinomaki,_Miyagi "Ishinomaki, Miyagi") – 3.3 meters (11 ft) or higher
* 15:21 JST – [Miyako](/wiki/Miyako,_Iwate "Miyako, Iwate") – 4 meters (13 ft) or higher
* 15:21 JST – [Kamaishi](/wiki/Kamaishi "Kamaishi") – 4.1 meters (13 ft) or higher
* 15:44 JST – [Erimo-cho Shoya](/wiki/Erimo,_Hokkaido "Erimo, Hokkaido") – 3.5 meters (11 ft)
* 15:50 JST – [Sōma](/wiki/S%C5%8Dma,_Fukushima "Sōma, Fukushima") – 7.3 meters (24 ft) or higher
* 16:52 JST – [Ōarai](/wiki/%C5%8Carai "Ōarai") – 4.2 meters (14 ft)

Many areas were also affected by waves of 1 to 3 meters (3 ft 3 in to 9 ft 10 in) in height, and the JMA bulletin also included the caveat that *"At some parts of the coasts, tsunamis may be higher than those observed at the observation sites."* The timing of the earliest recorded tsunami maximum readings ranged from 15:12 to 15:21, between 26 and 35 minutes after the earthquake had struck. The bulletin also included initial tsunami observation details, as well as more detailed maps for the coastlines affected by the tsunami waves.[[174]](#cite_note-176)[[175]](#cite_note-177)

JMA also reported offshore tsunami height recorded by [telemetry](/wiki/Telemetry "Telemetry") from [moored](/wiki/Mooring_(watercraft) "Mooring (watercraft)") [GPS](/wiki/GPS "GPS") wave-height meter buoys as follows:[[176]](#cite_note-178)

* offshore of central [Iwate](/wiki/Iwate_Prefecture "Iwate Prefecture") ([Miyako](/wiki/Miyako,_Iwate "Miyako, Iwate")) – 6.3 meters (21 ft)
* offshore of northern [Iwate](/wiki/Iwate_Prefecture "Iwate Prefecture") ([Kuji](/wiki/Kuji,_Iwate "Kuji, Iwate")) – 6 meters (20 ft)
* offshore of northern [Miyagi](/wiki/Miyagi_Prefecture "Miyagi Prefecture") ([Kesennuma](/wiki/Kesennuma "Kesennuma")) – 6 meters (20 ft)

On 25 March 2011, Port and Airport Research Institute (PARI) reported tsunami height by visiting the port sites as follows:[[177]](#cite_note-179)

* [Port of Hachinohe](/wiki/Port_of_Hachinohe "Port of Hachinohe") – 5–6 meters (16–20 ft)
* Port of Hachinohe area – 8–9 meters (26–30 ft)
* Port of [Kuji](/wiki/Kuji,_Iwate "Kuji, Iwate") – 8–9 meters (26–30 ft)
* Port of [Kamaishi](/wiki/Kamaishi,_Iwate "Kamaishi, Iwate") – 7–9 meters (23–30 ft)
* Port of [Ōfunato](/wiki/%C5%8Cfunato,_Iwate "Ōfunato, Iwate") – 9.5 meters (31 ft)
* Run up height, port of [Ōfunato](/wiki/%C5%8Cfunato,_Iwate "Ōfunato, Iwate") area – 24 meters (79 ft)
* Fishery port of [Onagawa](/wiki/Onagawa,_Miyagi "Onagawa, Miyagi") – 15 meters (49 ft)
* Port of [Ishinomaki](/wiki/Ishinomaki,_Miyagi "Ishinomaki, Miyagi") – 5 meters (16 ft)
* Shiogama section of [Shiogama](/wiki/Shiogama "Shiogama")-[Sendai](/wiki/Sendai "Sendai") port – 4 meters (13 ft)
* Sendai section of Shiogama-Sendai port – 8 meters (26 ft)
* [Sendai Airport](/wiki/Sendai_Airport "Sendai Airport") area – 12 meters (39 ft)

The tsunami at Ryōri Bay (綾里湾), Ōfunato reached a height of 40.1 meters (132 ft) (run-up elevation). Fishing equipment was scattered on the high cliff above the bay.[[178]](#cite_note-ofunato-180)[[179]](#cite_note-181) At [Tarō, Iwate](/wiki/Tar%C5%8D,_Iwate "Tarō, Iwate"), the tsunami reached a height of 37.9 meters (124 ft) up the slope of a mountain some 200 meters (660 ft) away from the [coastline](/wiki/Coast "Coast").[[180]](#cite_note-38-meter-182) Also, at the slope of a nearby mountain from 400 meters (1,300 ft) away at Aneyoshi fishery port (姉吉漁港) of Omoe peninsula (重茂半島) in [Miyako, Iwate](/wiki/Miyako,_Iwate "Miyako, Iwate"), [Tokyo University of Marine Science and Technology](/wiki/Tokyo_University_of_Marine_Science_and_Technology "Tokyo University of Marine Science and Technology") found estimated tsunami run up height of 38.9 meters (128 ft).[[37]](#cite_note-yomiuri-38) This height is deemed the record in Japan historically, as of reporting date, that exceeds 38.2 meters (125 ft) from the [1896 Sanriku earthquake](/wiki/1896_Sanriku_earthquake "1896 Sanriku earthquake").[[181]](#cite_note-183) It was also estimated that the tsunami reached heights of up to 40.5 meters (133 ft) in Miyako in Tōhoku's Iwate Prefecture. The inundated areas closely matched those of the [869 Sanriku tsunami](/wiki/869_Jogan_Sanriku_earthquake "869 Jogan Sanriku earthquake").[[182]](#cite_note-184)

[NOAA](/wiki/National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration "National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration") animation of the tsunami's propagation

Inundation heights were observed along 2,000 kilometers (1,200 mi) of the coast from Hokkaido to Kyushu in a 2012 study. Maximum [run-up](/wiki/Wave_run-up "Wave run-up") heights greater than 10 meters (33 ft) were distributed along 530 kilometers (330 mi) of coast, and maximum run-up heights greater than 20 meters (66 ft) were distributed along 200 kilometers (120 mi) of the coast, measured directly.[[183]](#cite_note-185) The tsunami resulted in significant erosion of the Rikuzen-Takata coastline, mainly caused by backwash. A 2016 study indicated that the coast has not naturally recovered at a desirable rate since the tsunami.[[184]](#cite_note-186)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/2011_Tohoku_earthquake_tsunami_in_Miyako.jpg/250px-2011_Tohoku_earthquake_tsunami_in_Miyako.jpg)](/wiki/File:2011_Tohoku_earthquake_tsunami_in_Miyako.jpg)

Tsunami in [Miyako, Iwate](/wiki/Miyako,_Iwate "Miyako, Iwate")

A Japanese government study found that 58% of people in coastal areas in Iwate, Miyagi, and Fukushima prefectures heeded tsunami warnings immediately after the quake and headed for higher ground. Of those who attempted to evacuate after hearing the warning, only five percent were caught in the tsunami. Of those who did not heed the warning, 49% were hit by the water.[[185]](#cite_note-187)

Delayed evacuations in response to the warnings had a number of causes. The tsunami height that had been initially predicted by the [tsunami warning system](/wiki/Tsunami_warning_system "Tsunami warning system") was lower than the actual tsunami height; this error contributed to the delayed escape of some residents. The discrepancy arose as follows: in order to produce a quick prediction of a tsunami's height and thus to provide a timely warning, the initial earthquake and tsunami warning that was issued for the event was based on a calculation that requires only about three minutes. This calculation is, in turn, based on the maximum amplitude of the seismic wave. The amplitude of the seismic wave is measured using the [JMA magnitude scale](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%9E%E3%82%B0%E3%83%8B%E3%83%81%E3%83%A5%E3%83%BC%E3%83%89#気象庁マグニチュード_Mj "ja:マグニチュード"), which is similar to [Richter scale](/wiki/Richter_scale "Richter scale"). However, these scales "saturate" for earthquakes that are above a certain magnitude (magnitude 8 on the JMA scale); that is, in the case of very large earthquakes, the scales' values change little despite large differences in the earthquakes' energy. This resulted in an underestimation of the tsunami's height in initial reports. Problems in issuing updates also contributed to delays in evacuations. The warning system was supposed to be updated about 15 minutes after the earthquake occurred, by which time the calculation for the [moment magnitude scale](/wiki/Moment_magnitude_scale "Moment magnitude scale") would normally be completed. However, the strong quake had exceeded the measurement limit of all of the [teleseismometers](/wiki/Seismometer#teleseismometer "Seismometer") within Japan, and thus it was impossible to calculate the moment magnitude based on data from those seismometers. Another cause of delayed evacuations was the release of the second update on the tsunami warning long after the earthquake (28 minutes, according to observations); by that time, power failures and similar circumstances reportedly prevented the update from reaching some residents. Also, observed data from tidal meters that were located off the coast were not fully reflected in the second warning. Furthermore, shortly after the earthquake, some wave meters reported a fluctuation of "20 centimeters (7.9 in)", and this value was broadcast throughout the mass media and the warning system, which caused some residents to underestimate the danger of their situation and even delayed or suspended their evacuation.[[186]](#cite_note-JMA20110912-188)[[187]](#cite_note-JMA2013-189)

In response to the aforementioned shortcomings in the tsunami warning system, JMA began an investigation in 2011 and updated their system in 2013. In the updated system, for a powerful earthquake that is capable of causing the JMA magnitude scale to saturate, no quantitative prediction will be released in the initial warning; instead, there will be words that describe the situation's emergency. There are plans to install new teleseismometers with the ability to measure larger earthquakes, which would allow the calculation of a quake's moment magnitude scale in a timely manner. JMA also implemented a simpler empirical method to integrate, into a tsunami warning, data from GPS tidal meters as well as from undersea water pressure meters, and there are plans to install more of these meters and to develop further technology to utilize data observed by them. To prevent under-reporting of tsunami heights, early quantitative observation data that are smaller than the expected amplitude will be overridden and the public will instead be told that the situation is under observation. About 90 seconds after an earthquake, an additional report on the possibility of a tsunami will also be included in observation reports, in order to warn people before the JMA magnitude can be calculated.[[186]](#cite_note-JMA20110912-188)[[187]](#cite_note-JMA2013-189)

### Elsewhere across the Pacific

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Midway_bonin_petrel_2011_tsunami.jpg/250px-Midway_bonin_petrel_2011_tsunami.jpg)](/wiki/File:Midway_bonin_petrel_2011_tsunami.jpg)

A [Bonin petrel](/wiki/Bonin_petrel "Bonin petrel") trapped in tsunami debris on [Midway Atoll](/wiki/Midway_Atoll "Midway Atoll") before being rescued

The [Pacific Tsunami Warning Center](/wiki/Pacific_Tsunami_Warning_Center "Pacific Tsunami Warning Center") (PTWC) in Hawaii issued tsunami watches and announcements for locations in the Pacific. At 07:30 UTC, PTWC issued a widespread tsunami warning covering the entire Pacific Ocean.[[188]](#cite_note-190)[[189]](#cite_note-191) Russia evacuated 11,000 residents from coastal areas of the [Kuril Islands](/wiki/Kuril_Islands "Kuril Islands").[[190]](#cite_note-192) The United States [National Tsunami Warning Center](/wiki/National_Tsunami_Warning_Center "National Tsunami Warning Center") issued a tsunami warning for the coastal areas in most of [California](/wiki/California "California"), all of [Oregon](/wiki/Oregon "Oregon"), and the western part of [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska"), and a tsunami advisory covering the Pacific coastlines of most of Alaska, and all of [Washington](/wiki/Washington_state "Washington state") and [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia"), Canada.[[191]](#cite_note-noaa6-193)[[192]](#cite_note-bctsunami-194) In [California](/wiki/California "California") and [Oregon](/wiki/Oregon "Oregon"), up to 2.4 m-high (7.9 ft) tsunami waves hit some areas, damaging docks and harbors and causing over US$10 million in damage.[[193]](#cite_note-195) In [Curry County, Oregon](/wiki/Curry_County,_Oregon "Curry County, Oregon"), US$7 million in damage occurred including the destruction of 1,100 m (3,600 ft) of docks at the [Brookings](/wiki/Brookings,_Oregon "Brookings, Oregon") harbor; the county received over US$1 million in [FEMA](/wiki/Federal_Emergency_Management_Agency "Federal Emergency Management Agency") emergency grants from the US Federal Government.[[194]](#cite_note-196) Surges of up to 1 m (3 ft 3 in) hit [Vancouver Island](/wiki/Vancouver_Island "Vancouver Island") in Canada[[192]](#cite_note-bctsunami-194)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] prompting some evacuations, and causing boats to be banned from the waters surrounding the island for 12 hours following the wave strike, leaving many island residents in the area without means of getting to work.[[195]](#cite_note-197)[[196]](#cite_note-198)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Boats_left_nearby_the_%27costanera%27_for_safety_in_Pichilemu%2C_March_11%2C_2011.jpg/250px-Boats_left_nearby_the_%27costanera%27_for_safety_in_Pichilemu%2C_March_11%2C_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boats_left_nearby_the_%27costanera%27_for_safety_in_Pichilemu,_March_11,_2011.jpg)

Fishing boats that were moved to higher ground in anticipation of tsunami arrival, in [Pichilemu](/wiki/Pichilemu "Pichilemu"), Chile

In the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), waves up to 0.5 m (1 ft 8 in) high hit the eastern seaboard of the country. Some houses along the coast in [Jayapura, Indonesia](/wiki/Jayapura,_Indonesia "Jayapura, Indonesia") were destroyed.[[197]](#cite_note-199) Authorities in [Wewak](/wiki/Wewak "Wewak"), [East Sepik](/wiki/East_Sepik "East Sepik"), Papua New Guinea evacuated 100 patients from the city's Boram Hospital before it was hit by waves, causing an estimated US$4 million in damage.[[198]](#cite_note-abcaus-200) Hawaii estimated damage to public infrastructure alone at US$3 million, with damage to private properties, including resort hotels such as [Four Seasons Resort Hualalai](/wiki/Four_Seasons_Resort_Hualalai "Four Seasons Resort Hualalai"), estimated at tens of millions of dollars.[[199]](#cite_note-Hawaii_Star_1-201) It was reported that a 1.5 m-high (4.9 ft) wave completely submerged [Midway Atoll](/wiki/Midway_Atoll "Midway Atoll")'s reef inlets and Spit Island, killing more than 110,000 nesting [seabirds](/wiki/Seabirds "Seabirds") at the [Midway Atoll National Wildlife Refuge](/wiki/Midway_Atoll_National_Wildlife_Refuge "Midway Atoll National Wildlife Refuge").[[200]](#cite_note-cnnmarch19-202) Some other South Pacific countries, including [Tonga](/wiki/Tonga "Tonga") and New Zealand, and US territories [American Samoa](/wiki/American_Samoa "American Samoa") and [Guam](/wiki/Guam "Guam"), experienced larger-than-normal waves, but did not report any major damage.[[201]](#cite_note-203) However, in Guam some roads were closed off and people were evacuated from low-lying areas.[[202]](#cite_note-204)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Japanese_2011_Tsunami_Boat.jpg/250px-Japanese_2011_Tsunami_Boat.jpg)](/wiki/File:Japanese_2011_Tsunami_Boat.jpg)

A Japanese Boat that washed ashore on the west coast of the United States, now preserved at the [Columbia River Maritime Museum](/wiki/Columbia_River_Maritime_Museum "Columbia River Maritime Museum")

Along the Pacific Coast of Mexico and South America, tsunami surges were reported, but in most places caused little or no damage.[[203]](#cite_note-channelnewsasia1-205) [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru") reported a wave of 1.5 m (4 ft 11 in) and more than 300 homes damaged.[[203]](#cite_note-channelnewsasia1-205) The surge in [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile") was large enough to damage more than 200 houses,[[204]](#cite_note-206) with waves of up to 3 m (9.8 ft).[[205]](#cite_note-207)[[206]](#cite_note-208) In the [Galápagos Islands](/wiki/Gal%C3%A1pagos_Islands "Galápagos Islands"), 260 families received assistance following a 3 m (9.8 ft) surge which arrived 20 hours after the earthquake, after the tsunami warning had been lifted.[[207]](#cite_note-NBR_88155-209)[[208]](#cite_note-210) There was a great deal of damage to buildings on the islands and one man was injured but there were no reported fatalities.[[207]](#cite_note-NBR_88155-209)[[209]](#cite_note-211)

After a 2 m (6 ft 7 in) high surge hit Chile, it was reported that the reflection from those surges traveled *back* across the Pacific, causing a 30–60 cm (12–24 in) surge in Japan, 47–48 hours after the earthquake, according to observation from multiple [tide gauges](/wiki/Tide_gauge "Tide gauge"), including in Onahama, Owase, and Kushimoto.[[210]](#cite_note-212)[[211]](#cite_note-213)

The tsunami broke [icebergs](/wiki/Iceberg "Iceberg") off the [Sulzberger Ice Shelf](/wiki/Sulzberger_Ice_Shelf "Sulzberger Ice Shelf") in [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica"), 13,000 km (8,100 mi) away. The main iceberg measured 9.5 km × 6.5 km (5.9 mi × 4.0 mi) (approximately the area of [Manhattan Island](/wiki/Manhattan_Island "Manhattan Island")) and about 80 m (260 ft) thick. A total of 125 km2 (48 sq mi; 31,000 acres) of ice broke away.[[212]](#cite_note-214)[[213]](#cite_note-215)

As of April 2012, wreckage from the tsunami spread around the world, including a [soccer](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") ball which was found in Alaska's [Middleton Island](/wiki/Middleton_Island "Middleton Island") and a Japanese motorcycle found in British Columbia, Canada.[[214]](#cite_note-216)[[215]](#cite_note-217)

### Norway

On the day of the earthquake, the waters of several [fjords](/wiki/Fjords "Fjords") across Norway appeared to seethe as if boiling, and formed waves that rolled onto shores, most prominently in [Sognefjorden](/wiki/Sognefjorden "Sognefjorden"), where the phenomenon was caught on film. After two years of research into the incidents in Norway, scientists concluded that the seismic energy of the earthquake generated [seiche](/wiki/Seiche "Seiche") waves thousands of kilometers away.[[216]](#cite_note-218)[[217]](#cite_note-219)

## Casualties

### Japan

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Operation_Tomodachi_in_Kamaishi.jpg/250px-Operation_Tomodachi_in_Kamaishi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Operation_Tomodachi_in_Kamaishi.jpg)

[VA-TF1](/wiki/Urban_Search_and_Rescue_Virginia_Task_Force_1 "Urban Search and Rescue Virginia Task Force 1") Rescue team from [Fairfax County, Virginia](/wiki/Fairfax_County,_Virginia "Fairfax County, Virginia") searching for survivors in [Kamaishi](/wiki/Kamaishi,_Iwate "Kamaishi, Iwate"), [Iwate Prefecture](/wiki/Iwate_Prefecture "Iwate Prefecture"). Snow arrived minutes before or after the tsunami, depending on locations.[[40]](#cite_note-GQ_Okawa_school-41)

#### Key statistics
