The official figures released in 2021 reported 19,759 deaths,[[220]](#cite_note-222) 6,242 injured,[[221]](#cite_note-223) and 2,553 people missing.[[222]](#cite_note-224) There were 10,567 deaths in [Miyagi](/wiki/Miyagi_Prefecture "Miyagi Prefecture"), 5,145 in [Iwate](/wiki/Iwate_Prefecture "Iwate Prefecture"), 3,920 in [Fukushima](/wiki/Fukushima_Prefecture "Fukushima Prefecture"), 66 in [Ibaraki](/wiki/Ibaraki_Prefecture "Ibaraki Prefecture"), 22 in [Chiba](/wiki/Chiba_Prefecture "Chiba Prefecture"), eight in [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"), six in [Kanagawa](/wiki/Kanagawa_Prefecture "Kanagawa Prefecture"), four in [Tochigi](/wiki/Tochigi_Prefecture "Tochigi Prefecture"), three each in [Aomori](/wiki/Aomori_Prefecture "Aomori Prefecture") and [Yamagata](/wiki/Yamagata_Prefecture "Yamagata Prefecture"), and one each in [Gunma](/wiki/Gunma_Prefecture "Gunma Prefecture"), [Saitama](/wiki/Saitama_Prefecture "Saitama Prefecture") and [Hokkaido](/wiki/Hokkaido "Hokkaido").[[42]](#cite_note-official_deaths_as_of_2021-03-09-43) The leading causes of death were drowning (90.64% or 14,308 bodies), burning (0.9% or 145 bodies) and others (4.2% or 667 bodies, mostly crushed by heavy objects).[[218]](#cite_note-Causes_of_death_20120311-220) Injuries related to [nuclear exposure](/wiki/Radiation_effects_from_the_Fukushima_Daiichi_nuclear_disaster "Radiation effects from the Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster") or the [discharge of radioactive water in Fukushima](/wiki/Discharge_of_radioactive_water_of_the_Fukushima_Daiichi_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Discharge of radioactive water of the Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Power Plant") are difficult to trace as 60% of the 20,000 workers on-site declined to participate in state-sponsored free health checks.[[223]](#cite_note-225)

Elderly aged over 60 account for 65.8% of all deaths, as shown on the table to the right.[[218]](#cite_note-Causes_of_death_20120311-220)

For the purpose of relief fund, an "earthquake-related death" was defined to include "Physical and mental fatigue caused by life in temporary shelter", "Physical and mental fatigue caused by evacuation", "Delayed treatment due to an inoperative hospital", "Physical and mental fatigue caused by stress from the earthquake and tsunami". A few cases of suicide are also included. Most of these deaths occurred during the first six months after the earthquake and the number dropped thereafter, but as time has passed, the number has continued to increase. Most of these deaths occurred in Fukushima Prefecture, where the prefectural government has suggested that they could be due to evacuations caused by the [Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster](/wiki/Fukushima_Daiichi_nuclear_disaster "Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster").[[224]](#cite_note-226) Within Fukushima Prefecture, these indirect casualties have already resulted in more deaths than the number of people killed directly by earthquake and tsunami.[[225]](#cite_note-227)[[226]](#cite_note-Smith-228)[[227]](#cite_note-mainichi.jp-229)

#### Others

[Save the Children](/wiki/Save_the_Children "Save the Children") reports that as many as 100,000 children were uprooted from their homes, some of whom were separated from their families because the earthquake occurred during the school day.[[228]](#cite_note-230) 236 children were orphaned in the prefectures of [Iwate](/wiki/Iwate_Prefecture "Iwate Prefecture"), [Miyagi](/wiki/Miyagi_Prefecture "Miyagi Prefecture") and [Fukushima](/wiki/Fukushima_Prefecture "Fukushima Prefecture") by the disaster;[[229]](#cite_note-231)[[230]](#cite_note-232) 1,580 children lost either one or both parents,[[231]](#cite_note-233) 846 in Miyagi, 572 in Iwate, and 162 in Fukushima.[[232]](#cite_note-234) The quake and tsunami killed 378 elementary, middle-school, and high school students and left 158 others missing.[[233]](#cite_note-235) One elementary school in [Ishinomaki, Miyagi](/wiki/Ishinomaki,_Miyagi "Ishinomaki, Miyagi"), [Okawa Elementary School](/wiki/Okawa_Elementary_School "Okawa Elementary School"), lost 74 of 108 students and 10 of 13 teachers in the tsunami due to poor decision making in evacuation.[[40]](#cite_note-GQ_Okawa_school-41)[[234]](#cite_note-236)[[235]](#cite_note-237)[[236]](#cite_note-238)

The Japanese Foreign Ministry confirmed the deaths of nineteen foreigners.[[237]](#cite_note-239) Among them were two English teachers from the United States affiliated with the [Japan Exchange and Teaching Program](/wiki/JET_Programme "JET Programme");[[238]](#cite_note-240) a Canadian missionary in [Shiogama](/wiki/Shiogama "Shiogama");[[239]](#cite_note-241) and citizens of China, North and South Korea, Taiwan, Pakistan and the Philippines.

By 9:30 UTC on 11 March 2011, [Google Person Finder](/wiki/Google_Person_Finder "Google Person Finder"), which was previously used in the [Haitian](/wiki/2010_Haiti_earthquake "2010 Haiti earthquake"), [Chilean](/wiki/2010_Chile_earthquake "2010 Chile earthquake"), and [Christchurch, New Zealand](/wiki/2011_Christchurch_earthquake "2011 Christchurch earthquake") earthquakes, was collecting information about survivors and their locations.[[240]](#cite_note-242)[[241]](#cite_note-243)

[Japanese funerals](/wiki/Japanese_funeral "Japanese funeral") are normally elaborate Buddhist ceremonies that entail cremation. The thousands of bodies, however, exceeded the capacity of available [crematoriums](/wiki/Crematorium "Crematorium") and [morgues](/wiki/Morgue "Morgue"), many of them damaged,[[242]](#cite_note-244)[[243]](#cite_note-nishikawa20110323-245) and there were shortages of both kerosene—each cremation requires 50 litres—and [dry ice](/wiki/Dry_ice "Dry ice") for preservation.[[244]](#cite_note-allen20110324-246) The single crematorium in [Higashimatsushima](/wiki/Higashimatsushima,_Miyagi "Higashimatsushima, Miyagi"), for example, could only handle four bodies a day, although hundreds were found there.[[245]](#cite_note-wines20110324-247) Governments and the military were forced to bury many bodies in hastily dug [mass graves](/wiki/Mass_grave "Mass grave") with rudimentary or no rites, although relatives of the deceased were promised that they would be cremated later.[[246]](#cite_note-ap20110327-248)

As of 27 May 2011, three [Japan Ground Self-Defense Force](/wiki/Japan_Ground_Self-Defense_Force "Japan Ground Self-Defense Force") members had died while conducting relief operations in Tōhoku.[[247]](#cite_note-249) As of March 2012, the Japanese government had recognized 1,331 deaths as indirectly related to the earthquake, such as caused by harsh living conditions after the disaster.[[248]](#cite_note-250) As of 30 April 2012, 18 people had died and 420 had been injured while participating in disaster recovery or clean-up efforts.[[249]](#cite_note-251)

### Overseas

The tsunami was reported to have caused several deaths outside Japan. One man was killed in [Jayapura](/wiki/Jayapura "Jayapura"), [Papua](/wiki/Papua_(province) "Papua (province)"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") after being swept out to sea.[[12]](#cite_note-Jakarta_Globe-12) A man who is said to have been attempting to photograph the oncoming tsunami at the mouth of the [Klamath River](/wiki/Klamath_River "Klamath River"), south of [Crescent City, California](/wiki/Crescent_City,_California "Crescent City, California"), was swept out to sea.[[250]](#cite_note-252) His body was found on 2 April 2011 along Ocean Beach in [Fort Stevens State Park](/wiki/Fort_Stevens_State_Park "Fort Stevens State Park"), Oregon, 530 km (330 mi) to the north.[[13]](#cite_note-BNO_News-13)

## Damage and effects

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Distant_view_of_Rikuzentakata.jpg/330px-Distant_view_of_Rikuzentakata.jpg)](/wiki/File:Distant_view_of_Rikuzentakata.jpg)

Panorama of Rikuzentakata

The degree and extent of damage caused by the earthquake and resulting tsunami were enormous, with most of the damage being caused by the tsunami. Video footage of the towns that were worst affected shows little more than piles of rubble, with almost no parts of any structures left standing.[[251]](#cite_note-253) Estimates of the cost of the damage range well into the tens of billions of [US dollars](/wiki/US_dollars "US dollars"); before-and-after [satellite](/wiki/Satellite "Satellite") photographs of devastated regions show immense damage.[[252]](#cite_note-254)[[253]](#cite_note-255) Although Japan has invested the equivalent of billions of dollars on anti-tsunami seawalls which line at least 40% of its 34,751 km (21,593 mi) coastline and stand up to 12 m (39 ft) high, the tsunami simply washed over the top of some seawalls, collapsing some in the process.[[254]](#cite_note-256)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Effect_of_2011_Sendai_earthquake_in_Tokyo_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Effect_of_2011_Sendai_earthquake_in_Tokyo_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Effect_of_2011_Sendai_earthquake_in_Tokyo_(cropped).jpg)

A fire that broke out in [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo")

Japan's [National Police Agency](/wiki/National_Police_Agency_(Japan) "National Police Agency (Japan)") said on 3 April 2011, that 45,700 buildings were destroyed and 144,300 were damaged by the quake and tsunami. The damaged buildings included 29,500 structures in Miyagi Prefecture, 12,500 in Iwate Prefecture and 2,400 in Fukushima Prefecture.[[255]](#cite_note-257) Three hundred hospitals with 20 beds or more in Tōhoku were damaged by the disaster, with 11 being completely destroyed.[[256]](#cite_note-258) The earthquake and tsunami created an estimated 24–25 million tons[*[vague](/wiki/Wikipedia:Vagueness "Wikipedia:Vagueness")*] of rubble and debris in Japan.[[257]](#cite_note-259)[[258]](#cite_note-260)

A report by the National Police Agency of Japan on 10 September 2018 listed 121,778 buildings as "total collapsed", with a further 280,926 buildings "half collapsed", and another 699,180 buildings "partially damaged".[[259]](#cite_note-npajproperty-261) The earthquake and tsunami also caused extensive and severe structural damage in north-eastern Japan, including heavy damage to roads and railways as well as fires in many areas, and a dam collapse.[[39]](#cite_note-roland_buerk-40)[[260]](#cite_note-262) [Japanese Prime Minister](/wiki/Japanese_Prime_Minister "Japanese Prime Minister") [Naoto Kan](/wiki/Naoto_Kan "Naoto Kan") said, "In the 65 years after the end of World War II, this is the toughest and the most difficult crisis for Japan."[[261]](#cite_note-263) Around 4.4 million households in northeastern Japan were left without electricity and 1.5 million without water.[[262]](#cite_note-waterfoodheat-264)

An estimated 230,000 automobiles and trucks were damaged or destroyed in the disaster. As of the end of May 2011, residents of Iwate, Miyagi, and Fukushima prefectures had requested the deregistration of 15,000 vehicles, meaning that the owners of those vehicles were writing them off as unrepairable or unsalvageable.[[263]](#cite_note-265)

### Weather conditions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Ishinomaki_in_snow.jpg/250px-Ishinomaki_in_snow.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ishinomaki_in_snow.jpg)

Snowfall in [Ishinomaki](/wiki/Ishinomaki "Ishinomaki"), 16 March. Snow fell across several areas where the tsunami struck, further worsening the conditions and hampering rescue efforts.

Low temperature and [snowfall](/wiki/Snowfall "Snowfall") were major concerns after the earthquake.[[264]](#cite_note-Snow_piles_more_misery-266) Snow arrived minutes before or after the tsunami, depending on locations.[[40]](#cite_note-GQ_Okawa_school-41) In [Ishinomaki](/wiki/Ishinomaki "Ishinomaki"), the city which suffered the most deaths,[[42]](#cite_note-official_deaths_as_of_2021-03-09-43) a temperature of 0 °C (32 °F) was measured, and it began to snow within a couple of hours of the earthquake.[[43]](#cite_note-weather_ishinomaki-44)[[40]](#cite_note-GQ_Okawa_school-41) Major snow fell again on 16 March,[[41]](#cite_note-asahi_20110316-42)[[265]](#cite_note-267) and intermittently in the coming weeks.[[266]](#cite_note-Sendai_daily_values_in_March-268) 18 March was the coldest of that month, recording −4 to 6 °C (25 to 43 °F) in [Sendai](/wiki/Sendai "Sendai").[[266]](#cite_note-Sendai_daily_values_in_March-268)[[41]](#cite_note-asahi_20110316-42) Photos of city ruins covered with snow were featured in various photo albums in international media, including [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA").[[267]](#cite_note-269)[[268]](#cite_note-270)[[269]](#cite_note-271)

### Waste

The tsunami produced huge amounts of debris: estimates of 5 million tonnes of waste were reported by the [Japanese Ministry of the Environment](/wiki/Ministry_of_the_Environment_(Japan) "Ministry of the Environment (Japan)"). Some of this waste, mostly plastic and [styrofoam](/wiki/Styrofoam "Styrofoam"), washed up on the coasts of Canada and the United States in late 2011. Along the west coast of the United States, this increased the amount of litter by a factor of ten and may have transported [alien species](/wiki/Introduced_species "Introduced species").[[270]](#cite_note-:12-272)

### Ports

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Ship_on_the_port_CROP.jpg/250px-Ship_on_the_port_CROP.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ship_on_the_port_CROP.jpg)

A damaged crane stands next to a ship lifted onto the docks at [Sendai](/wiki/Sendai "Sendai")'s port.

All of Japan's ports were briefly shut down after the earthquake, though the ones in Tokyo and southwards soon re-opened. Fifteen ports were located in the disaster zone. The north eastern ports of Hachinohe, Sendai, Ishinomaki and Onahama were destroyed, while the [Port of Chiba](/wiki/Port_of_Chiba "Port of Chiba") (which serves the hydrocarbon industry) and Japan's ninth-largest container port at [Kashima](/wiki/Kashima,_Ibaraki "Kashima, Ibaraki") were also affected, though less severely. The ports at Hitachinaka, Hitachi, Soma, Shiogama, Kesennuma, Ofunato, Kamashi and Miyako were also damaged and closed to ships.[[271]](#cite_note-273) All 15 ports reopened to limited ship traffic by 29 March 2011.[[272]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-274) A total of 319 fishing ports, about 10% of Japan's fishing ports, were damaged in the disaster.[[273]](#cite_note-275) Most were restored to operating condition by 18 April 2012.[[274]](#cite_note-276)

The [Port of Tokyo](/wiki/Port_of_Tokyo "Port of Tokyo") suffered slight damage; the effects of the quake included visible smoke rising from a building in the port with parts of the port areas being flooded, including [soil liquefaction](/wiki/Soil_liquefaction "Soil liquefaction") in [Tokyo Disneyland](/wiki/Tokyo_Disneyland "Tokyo Disneyland")'s parking lot.[[275]](#cite_note-CNA0-277)[[276]](#cite_note-CNA1-278)

### Dams and water problems

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Fujinuma_Dam_failure.JPG/250px-Fujinuma_Dam_failure.JPG)](/wiki/File:Fujinuma_Dam_failure.JPG)

Dam failure at Fujinuma

The [Fujinuma irrigation dam](/wiki/Fujinuma_Dam "Fujinuma Dam") in [Sukagawa](/wiki/Sukagawa "Sukagawa") ruptured,[[277]](#cite_note-The_Gulf_Today-279) causing flooding and the washing away of five homes.[[278]](#cite_note-280) Eight people were missing and four bodies were discovered by the morning.[[279]](#cite_note-281)[[280]](#cite_note-282)[[281]](#cite_note-283) Reportedly, some locals had attempted to repair leaks in the dam before it completely [failed](/wiki/Dam_failure "Dam failure").[[282]](#cite_note-284) On 12 March 252 dams were inspected and it was discovered that six [embankment dams](/wiki/Embankment_dam "Embankment dam") had shallow cracks on their crests. The reservoir at one concrete [gravity dam](/wiki/Gravity_dam "Gravity dam") suffered a small non-serious [slope failure](/wiki/Slope_stability "Slope stability"). All damaged dams are functioning with no problems. Four dams within the quake area were unreachable.[[283]](#cite_note-285)

In the immediate aftermath of the calamity, at least 1.5 million households were reported to have lost access to water supplies.[[262]](#cite_note-waterfoodheat-264)[[284]](#cite_note-286) By 21 March 2011, this number fell to 1.04 million.[[285]](#cite_note-Reuters_figures-287)

### Electricity

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Power_Grid_of_Japan.svg/250px-Power_Grid_of_Japan.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Power_Grid_of_Japan.svg)

Geographic divide between 50 hertz systems and 60 hertz systems in Japan's electricity distribution network

According to the Japanese trade ministry, around 4.4 million households served by [Tōhoku Electric Power](/wiki/T%C5%8Dhoku_Electric_Power "Tōhoku Electric Power") (TEP) in northeastern Japan were left without electricity.[[286]](#cite_note-288) Several nuclear and conventional power plants went offline, reducing the [Tokyo Electric Power Company](/wiki/Tokyo_Electric_Power_Company "Tokyo Electric Power Company")'s (TEPCO) total capacity by 21 GW.[[287]](#cite_note-289) [Rolling blackouts](/wiki/Rolling_blackout "Rolling blackout") began on 14 March due to power shortages caused by the earthquake.[[288]](#cite_note-290) TEPCO, which normally provides approximately 40 [GW](/wiki/Gigawatt "Gigawatt") of electricity, announced that it could only provide about 30 GW, because 40% of the electricity used in the greater Tokyo area was supplied by reactors in the [Niigata](/wiki/Niigata_Prefecture "Niigata Prefecture") and [Fukushima](/wiki/Fukushima_Prefecture "Fukushima Prefecture") prefectures.[[289]](#cite_note-291) The reactors at the [Fukushima Daiichi](/wiki/Fukushima_Daiichi_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Power Plant") and [Fukushima Dai-ni](/wiki/Fukushima_Daini_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Fukushima Daini Nuclear Power Plant") plants were automatically taken offline when the first earthquake occurred and sustained major damage from the earthquake and subsequent tsunami. Rolling blackouts of approximately three hours were experienced throughout April and May while TEPCO scrambled to find a temporary power solution. The blackouts affected Tokyo, [Kanagawa](/wiki/Kanagawa_Prefecture "Kanagawa Prefecture"), Eastern [Shizuoka](/wiki/Shizuoka_Prefecture "Shizuoka Prefecture"), [Yamanashi](/wiki/Yamanashi_Prefecture "Yamanashi Prefecture"), [Chiba](/wiki/Chiba_Prefecture "Chiba Prefecture"), [Ibaraki](/wiki/Ibaraki_Prefecture "Ibaraki Prefecture"), [Saitama](/wiki/Saitama_Prefecture "Saitama Prefecture"), [Tochigi](/wiki/Tochigi_Prefecture "Tochigi Prefecture"), and [Gunma](/wiki/Gunma_Prefecture "Gunma Prefecture") prefectures.[[290]](#cite_note-292) Voluntary reductions in electricity use by consumers in the Kanto area helped reduce the predicted frequency and duration of the blackouts.[[291]](#cite_note-293) By 21 March 2011, the number of households in the north without electricity fell to 242,927.[[285]](#cite_note-Reuters_figures-287)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Devastation_in_Minamis%C5%8Dma_after_tsunami.jpg/250px-Devastation_in_Minamis%C5%8Dma_after_tsunami.jpg)](/wiki/File:Devastation_in_Minamis%C5%8Dma_after_tsunami.jpg)

A damaged [transmission tower](/wiki/Transmission_tower "Transmission tower") and severed power lines in [Minamisōma, Fukushima](/wiki/Minamis%C5%8Dma,_Fukushima "Minamisōma, Fukushima")

Tōhoku Electric Power was not able to provide the Kanto region with additional power because TEP's power plants were also damaged in the earthquake. [Kansai Electric Power Company](/wiki/Kansai_Electric_Power_Company "Kansai Electric Power Company") (Kepco) could not share electricity, because its system operated at 60 hertz, whereas TEPCO and TEP operate their systems at 50 hertz; the disparity is due to early industrial and infrastructure development in the 1880s that left Japan without a unified national [power grid](/wiki/Power_grid "Power grid").[[292]](#cite_note-294) Two substations, one in Shizuoka Prefecture and one in Nagano Prefecture, were able to convert between frequencies and transfer electricity from Kansai to Kanto and Tōhoku, but their capacity was limited to 1 GW. With damage to so many power plants, it was feared it might be years before a long-term solution could be found.[[293]](#cite_note-295)

To help alleviate the shortage, three steel manufacturers in the Kanto region contributed electricity produced by their in-house conventional power stations to TEPCO for distribution to the general public. [Sumitomo Metal Industries](/wiki/Sumitomo_Metal_Industries "Sumitomo Metal Industries") could produce up to 500 MW, [JFE Steel](/wiki/JFE_Holdings "JFE Holdings") 400 MW, and [Nippon Steel](/wiki/Nippon_Steel "Nippon Steel") 500 MW of electric power.[[294]](#cite_note-296) Auto and auto parts makers in Kanto and Tōhoku agreed in May 2011 to operate their factories on Saturdays and Sundays and close on Thursdays and Fridays to help alleviate electricity shortages during the summer of 2011.[[295]](#cite_note-297) The public and other companies were also encouraged to conserve electricity in the 2011 summer months ([Setsuden](/wiki/Setsuden "Setsuden")).[[296]](#cite_note-298)

The expected electricity crisis in 2011 summer was successfully prevented thanks to all the *setsuden* measures. Peak electricity consumption recorded by TEPCO during the period was 49.22GW, which is 10.77GW (18%) lower than the peak consumption in the previous year. Overall electricity consumption during July and August was also 14% less than in the previous year.[[297]](#cite_note-299) The peak electricity consumption within TEP's area was 12.46GW during the 2011 summer, 3.11GW (20%) less than the peak consumption in the previous year, and the overall consumption have been reduced by 11% in July with 17% in August compared to previous year.[[298]](#cite_note-300)[[299]](#cite_note-301)[[300]](#cite_note-TohokuSepMonthlyPress-302) The Japanese government continued to ask the public to conserve electricity until 2016, when it is predicted that the supply will be sufficient to meet demand, [*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] thanks to the deepening of the mindset to conserve electricity among corporate and general public, addition of new electricity providers due to the [electricity liberalization](/wiki/Energy_liberalization "Energy liberalization") policy, increased output from renewable energy as well as fossil fuel power stations, as well as sharing of electricity between different electricity companies.[[301]](#cite_note-303)[[302]](#cite_note-304)[[303]](#cite_note-305)

### Oil, gas and coal

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Cosmo_Oil_explosion_2_20110311CROP.jpg/250px-Cosmo_Oil_explosion_2_20110311CROP.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cosmo_Oil_explosion_2_20110311CROP.jpg)

Fire at the Cosmo Oil refinery in [Ichihara](/wiki/Ichihara,_Chiba "Ichihara, Chiba")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/SH-60B_helicopter_flies_over_Sendai.jpg/250px-SH-60B_helicopter_flies_over_Sendai.jpg)](/wiki/File:SH-60B_helicopter_flies_over_Sendai.jpg)

An aerial view of the smoke from the Sendai [Nippon Oil](/wiki/JX_Nippon_Oil_%26_Energy "JX Nippon Oil & Energy") refinery

A 220,000-barrel-per-day (35,000 m3)[[304]](#cite_note-Fernandez-306) [oil refinery](/wiki/Oil_refinery "Oil refinery") of [Cosmo Oil Company](/wiki/Cosmo_Oil_Company "Cosmo Oil Company") was set on fire by the quake at [Ichihara](/wiki/Ichihara,_Chiba "Ichihara, Chiba"), [Chiba Prefecture](/wiki/Chiba_Prefecture "Chiba Prefecture"), to the east of Tokyo.[[305]](#cite_note-307)[[306]](#cite_note-308) It was extinguished after ten days, injuring six people, and destroying storage tanks.[[307]](#cite_note-309) Other refineries halted production due to safety checks and power loss.[[308]](#cite_note-310)[[309]](#cite_note-311)
In Sendai, a 145,000-barrel-per-day (23,100 m3) refinery owned by the largest refiner in Japan, [JX Nippon Oil & Energy](/wiki/Nippon_Oil "Nippon Oil"), was also set ablaze by the quake.[[304]](#cite_note-Fernandez-306) Workers were evacuated,[[310]](#cite_note-312) but tsunami warnings hindered efforts to extinguish the fire until 14 March, when officials planned to do so.[[304]](#cite_note-Fernandez-306)

An analyst estimated that consumption of various types of oil could have increased by as much as 300,000 barrels (48,000 m3) per day (as well as LNG), as back-up power plants burning [fossil fuels](/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel") attempted to compensate for the loss of 11 GW of Japan's nuclear power capacity.[[311]](#cite_note-313)[[312]](#cite_note-314)

The city-owned plant for importing [liquefied natural gas](/wiki/Liquefied_natural_gas "Liquefied natural gas") in Sendai was severely damaged, and supplies were halted for at least a month.[[313]](#cite_note-315)

In addition to refining and storage, several power plants were damaged. These include Sendai #4, New-Sendai #1 and #2, Haranomachi #1 and #2, Hirono #2 and #4 and Hitachinaka #1.[[314]](#cite_note-316)

### Nuclear power plants

Further information: [Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster](/wiki/Fukushima_Daiichi_nuclear_disaster "Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster")

The [Fukushima Daiichi](/wiki/Fukushima_Daiichi_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Power Plant"), [Fukushima Daini](/wiki/Fukushima_Daini_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Fukushima Daini Nuclear Power Plant"), [Onagawa Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Onagawa_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Onagawa Nuclear Power Plant") and [Tōkai nuclear power stations](/wiki/T%C5%8Dkai_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Tōkai Nuclear Power Plant"), consisting of a total eleven reactors, were [automatically shut down](/wiki/Scram "Scram") following the earthquake.[[315]](#cite_note-11plants-317) [Higashidōri](/wiki/Higashid%C5%8Dri_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Higashidōri Nuclear Power Plant"), also on the northeast coast, was already shut down for a periodic inspection. Cooling is needed to remove [decay heat](/wiki/Decay_heat "Decay heat") after a [Generation II reactor](/wiki/Generation_II_reactor "Generation II reactor") has been shut down, and to maintain spent fuel pools. The backup cooling process is powered by emergency diesel generators at the plants and at [Rokkasho nuclear reprocessing plant](/wiki/Rokkasho_Reprocessing_Plant "Rokkasho Reprocessing Plant").[[316]](#cite_note-nei-20110311-318) At Fukushima Daiichi and Daini, tsunami waves overtopped seawalls and destroyed diesel backup power systems, leading to severe problems at Fukushima Daiichi, including three large explosions and radioactive leakage. Subsequent analysis found that many Japanese nuclear plants, including Fukushima Daiichi, were not adequately protected against tsunamis.[[317]](#cite_note-319) Over 200,000 people were evacuated.[[318]](#cite_note-320)

The [discharge of radioactive water in Fukushima](/wiki/Discharge_of_radioactive_water_of_the_Fukushima_Daiichi_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Discharge of radioactive water of the Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Power Plant") was confirmed in later analysis at the three reactors at [Fukushima I](/wiki/Fukushima_I "Fukushima I") (Units 1, 2, and 3), which suffered meltdowns and continued to leak coolant water.[[49]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-50)

The aftershock on 7 April caused the loss of external power to Rokkasho Reprocessing Plant and Higashidori Nuclear Power Plant but backup generators were functional. [Onagawa Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Onagawa_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Onagawa Nuclear Power Plant") lost three of its four external power lines and temporarily lost cooling function in its spent fuel pools for "20 to 80 minutes". A spill of "up to 3.8 litres [1.0 US gal]" of radioactive water also occurred at Onagawa following the aftershock.[[319]](#cite_note-ibtimes1-321) A report by the [IAEA](/wiki/IAEA "IAEA") in 2012 found that the [Onagawa Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Onagawa_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Onagawa Nuclear Power Plant") had remained largely undamaged.[[320]](#cite_note-322)

In 2013, only two nuclear reactors in Japan had been restarted since the 2011 shutdowns.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In February 2019, there were 42 operable reactors in Japan. Of these, only nine reactors in five power plants were operating after having been restarted post-2011.[[321]](#cite_note-323)

#### Fukushima meltdowns

Main article: [Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster](/wiki/Fukushima_Daiichi_nuclear_disaster "Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/VOA_Herman_-_April_12_2011_Namie-11.jpg/250px-VOA_Herman_-_April_12_2011_Namie-11.jpg)](/wiki/File:VOA_Herman_-_April_12_2011_Namie-11.jpg)

A loose cow roaming through [Namie, Fukushima](/wiki/Namie,_Fukushima "Namie, Fukushima") after the area was evacuated

Japan declared a state of emergency following the failure of the cooling system at the [Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Fukushima_Daiichi_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Power Plant"), resulting in the evacuation of nearby residents.[[322]](#cite_note-guardian2011-03-11-324)[[323]](#cite_note-325)[[324]](#cite_note-BBC3-326) Officials from the Japanese [Nuclear and Industrial Safety Agency](/wiki/Nuclear_and_Industrial_Safety_Agency "Nuclear and Industrial Safety Agency") reported that radiation levels inside the plant were up to 1,000 times normal levels,[[325]](#cite_note-327) and that radiation levels outside the plant were up to eight times normal levels.[[326]](#cite_note-328) Later, a state of emergency was also declared at the [Fukushima Daini](/wiki/Fukushima_Daini_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Fukushima Daini Nuclear Power Plant") nuclear power plant about 11 km (6.8 mi) south.[[327]](#cite_note-329) Experts described the Fukushima disaster was not as bad as the [Chernobyl disaster](/wiki/Chernobyl_disaster "Chernobyl disaster"), but worse than the [Three Mile Island accident](/wiki/Three_Mile_Island_accident "Three Mile Island accident").[[328]](#cite_note-330)

The [discharge of radioactive water of the Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Discharge_of_radioactive_water_of_the_Fukushima_Daiichi_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Discharge of radioactive water of the Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Power Plant") was later detected in tap water. Radioactive iodine was detected in the tap water in Fukushima, Tochigi, Gunma, Tokyo, Chiba, Saitama, and Niigata, and radioactive caesium in the tap water in Fukushima, Tochigi and Gunma.[[329]](#cite_note-331)[[330]](#cite_note-332)[[331]](#cite_note-333) Radioactive caesium, iodine, and strontium[[332]](#cite_note-334) were also detected in the soil in some places in Fukushima. There may be a need to replace the contaminated soil.[[333]](#cite_note-335) Many radioactive hotspots were found outside the evacuation zone, including Tokyo.[[334]](#cite_note-336) [Radioactive contamination of food products](/wiki/Radiation_effects_from_the_Fukushima_Daiichi_nuclear_disaster "Radiation effects from the Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster") were detected in several places in Japan.[[335]](#cite_note-337) In 2021, the Japanese cabinet finally approved the [dumping of radioactive water in Fukushima](/wiki/Discharge_of_radioactive_water_of_the_Fukushima_Daiichi_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Discharge of radioactive water of the Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Power Plant") into the Pacific Ocean over a course of 30 years, with full support of IAEA.[[336]](#cite_note-BBC_20210413-338)

#### Incidents elsewhere

A fire occurred in the turbine section of the [Onagawa Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Onagawa_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Onagawa Nuclear Power Plant") following the earthquake.[[316]](#cite_note-nei-20110311-318)[[337]](#cite_note-339) The blaze was in a building housing the turbine, which is sited separately from the plant's reactor,[[322]](#cite_note-guardian2011-03-11-324) and was soon extinguished.[[338]](#cite_note-Australian-340) The plant was shut down as a precaution.[[339]](#cite_note-341)

On 13 March the lowest-level state of emergency was declared regarding the Onagawa plant as radioactivity readings temporarily[[340]](#cite_note-342) exceeded allowed levels in the area of the plant.[[341]](#cite_note-343)[[342]](#cite_note-344) Tōhoku Electric Power Co. stated this may have been due to radiation from the Fukushima Daiichi nuclear accidents but was not from the Onagawa plant itself.[[343]](#cite_note-345)

As a result of the 7 April aftershock, Onagawa Nuclear Power Plant lost three of four external power lines and lost cooling function for as much as 80 minutes. A spill of a couple of litres of radioactive water occurred at Onagawa.[[319]](#cite_note-ibtimes1-321)

The number 2 reactor at [Tōkai Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/T%C5%8Dkai_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Tōkai Nuclear Power Plant") was shut down automatically.[[315]](#cite_note-11plants-317) On 14 March it was reported that a cooling system pump for this reactor had stopped working;[[344]](#cite_note-346) however, the Japan Atomic Power Company stated that there was a second operational pump sustaining the cooling systems, but that two of three diesel generators used to power the cooling system were out of order.[[345]](#cite_note-347)

### Transport

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Rokko-Bridge_fell%2CNamegata-city%2CJapan.jpg/250px-Rokko-Bridge_fell%2CNamegata-city%2CJapan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rokko-Bridge_fell,Namegata-city,Japan.jpg)

A highway bridge damaged and severed

Japan's transport network suffered severe disruptions. Many sections of [Tōhoku Expressway](/wiki/T%C5%8Dhoku_Expressway "Tōhoku Expressway") serving northern Japan were damaged. The expressway did not reopen to general public use until 24 March 2011.[[346]](#cite_note-348)[[347]](#cite_note-349) All railway services were suspended in Tokyo, with an estimated 20,000 people stranded at major stations across the city.[[348]](#cite_note-350) In the hours after the earthquake, some train services were resumed.[[349]](#cite_note-351) Most Tokyo area train lines resumed full service by the next day—12 March.[[350]](#cite_note-352) Twenty thousand stranded visitors spent the night of 11–12 March inside [Tokyo Disneyland](/wiki/Tokyo_Disneyland "Tokyo Disneyland").[[351]](#cite_note-353)

A tsunami flooded [Sendai Airport](/wiki/Sendai_Airport "Sendai Airport") at 15:55 JST,[[145]](#cite_note-AVH1-147) about one hour after the initial quake, causing severe damage. [Narita](/wiki/Narita_International_Airport "Narita International Airport") and [Haneda Airport](/wiki/Haneda_Airport "Haneda Airport") both briefly suspended operations after the quake, but suffered little damage and reopened within 24 hours.[[276]](#cite_note-CNA1-278) Eleven airliners bound for Narita were diverted to nearby [Yokota Air Base](/wiki/Yokota_Air_Base "Yokota Air Base").[[352]](#cite_note-354)[[353]](#cite_note-355)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Shinchi_Sta_20110404.jpg/250px-Shinchi_Sta_20110404.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shinchi_Sta_20110404.jpg)

Remains of [Shinchi Station](/wiki/Shinchi_Station "Shinchi Station")

Various train services around Japan were also canceled, with [JR East](/wiki/JR_East "JR East") suspending all services for the rest of the day.[[354]](#cite_note-356) Four trains on coastal lines were reported as being out of contact with operators; one, a four-car train on the [Senseki Line](/wiki/Senseki_Line "Senseki Line"), was found to have derailed, and its occupants were rescued shortly after 8 am the next morning.[[355]](#cite_note-357) [Minami-Kesennuma Station](/wiki/Minami-Kesennuma_Station "Minami-Kesennuma Station") on the [Kesennuma Line](/wiki/Kesennuma_Line "Kesennuma Line") was obliterated save for its platform;[[356]](#cite_note-358) 62 of 70 (31 of 35) JR East train lines suffered damage to some degree;[[272]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-274) in the worst-hit areas, 23 stations on 7 lines were washed away, with damage or loss of track in 680 locations and the 30-km radius around the Fukushima Daiichi nuclear plant unable to be assessed.[[357]](#cite_note-359)

There were no derailments of [Shinkansen](/wiki/Shinkansen "Shinkansen") bullet train services in and out of Tokyo, but their services were also suspended.[[276]](#cite_note-CNA1-278) The [Tokaido Shinkansen](/wiki/Tokaido_Shinkansen "Tokaido Shinkansen") resumed limited service late in the day and was back to its normal schedule by the next day, while the [Jōetsu](/wiki/J%C5%8Detsu_Shinkansen "Jōetsu Shinkansen") and [Nagano Shinkansen](/wiki/Nagano_Shinkansen "Nagano Shinkansen") resumed services late on 12 March. Services on [Yamagata Shinkansen](/wiki/Yamagata_Shinkansen "Yamagata Shinkansen") resumed with limited numbers of trains on 31 March.[[358]](#cite_note-360)

Derailments were minimized because of an [early warning system that detected the earthquake](/wiki/Earthquake_warning_system "Earthquake warning system") before it struck. The system automatically stopped all high-speed trains, which minimized the damage.[[359]](#cite_note-361)

The [Tōhoku Shinkansen](/wiki/T%C5%8Dhoku_Shinkansen "Tōhoku Shinkansen") line was worst hit, with JR East estimating that 1,100 sections of the line, varying from collapsed station roofs to bent power pylons, would need repairs. Services on the Tōhoku Shinkansen partially resumed only in Kantō area on 15 March, with one round-trip service per hour between [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo_Station "Tokyo Station") and [Nasu-Shiobara](/wiki/Nasushiobara_Station "Nasushiobara Station"),[[360]](#cite_note-362) and Tōhoku area service partially resumed on 22 March between [Morioka](/wiki/Morioka_Station "Morioka Station") and [Shin-Aomori](/wiki/Shin-Aomori_Station "Shin-Aomori Station").[[361]](#cite_note-363) Services on [Akita Shinkansen](/wiki/Akita_Shinkansen "Akita Shinkansen") resumed with limited numbers of trains on 18 March.[[362]](#cite_note-364) Service between Tokyo and [Shin-Aomori](/wiki/Shin-Aomori_Station "Shin-Aomori Station") was restored by May, but at lower speeds due to ongoing restoration work; the pre-earthquake timetable was not reinstated until late September.[[363]](#cite_note-365)

The rolling blackouts brought on by the crises at the nuclear power plants in Fukushima had a profound effect on the rail networks around Tokyo starting on 14 March. Major railways began running trains at 10–20 minute intervals, rather than the usual 3–5 minute intervals, operating some lines only at rush hour and completely shutting down others; notably, the [Tōkaidō Main Line](/wiki/T%C5%8Dkaid%C5%8D_Main_Line "Tōkaidō Main Line"), [Yokosuka Line](/wiki/Yokosuka_Line "Yokosuka Line"), [Sōbu Main Line](/wiki/S%C5%8Dbu_Main_Line "Sōbu Main Line") and [Chūō-Sōbu Line](/wiki/Ch%C5%AB%C5%8D-S%C5%8Dbu_Line "Chūō-Sōbu Line") were all stopped for the day.[[364]](#cite_note-366) This led to near-paralysis within the capital, with long lines at train stations and many people unable to come to work or get home. Railway operators gradually increased capacity over the next few days, until running at approximately 80% capacity by 17 March and relieving the worst of the passenger congestion.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Carried_train_in_Ishinomaki_Line.JPG/250px-Carried_train_in_Ishinomaki_Line.JPG)](/wiki/File:Carried_train_in_Ishinomaki_Line.JPG)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Fallen_power_poles_in_Ishinomaki.jpg/250px-Fallen_power_poles_in_Ishinomaki.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fallen_power_poles_in_Ishinomaki.jpg)

A [KiHa 40 series](/wiki/KiHa_40_series "KiHa 40 series") train washed away uphill from [Onagawa Station](/wiki/Onagawa_Station "Onagawa Station") (left) and a damaged utility pole in Ishinomaki (right).

### Telecommunications

Cellular and landline phone service suffered major disruptions in the affected area.[[365]](#cite_note-367) Immediately after the earthquake cellular communication was jammed across much of Japan due to a surge of network activity. On the day of the quake itself American broadcaster [NPR](/wiki/NPR "NPR") was unable to reach anyone in Sendai with a working phone or access to the Internet.[[366]](#cite_note-368) Internet services were largely unaffected in areas where basic infrastructure remained, despite the earthquake having damaged portions of several [undersea cable](/wiki/Submarine_communications_cable "Submarine communications cable") systems landing in the affected regions; these systems were able to reroute around affected segments onto redundant links.[[367]](#cite_note-369)[[368]](#cite_note-370) Within Japan, only a few websites were initially unreachable.[[369]](#cite_note-computerworld-371) Several [Wi-Fi](/wiki/Wi-Fi "Wi-Fi") hotspot providers reacted to the quake by providing free access to their networks,[[369]](#cite_note-computerworld-371) and some American telecommunications and [VoIP](/wiki/VoIP "VoIP") companies such as [AT&T](/wiki/AT%26T "AT&T"), [Sprint](/wiki/Sprint_Nextel "Sprint Nextel"), [Verizon](/wiki/Verizon "Verizon"),[[370]](#cite_note-372) [T-Mobile](/wiki/T-Mobile_International_AG "T-Mobile International AG")[[371]](#cite_note-373) and VoIP companies such as netTALK[[372]](#cite_note-374) and [Vonage](/wiki/Vonage "Vonage")[[373]](#cite_note-375) have offered free calls to (and in some cases, from) Japan for a limited time, as did Germany's [Deutsche Telekom](/wiki/Deutsche_Telekom "Deutsche Telekom").[[374]](#cite_note-376)

### Defense

[Matsushima Air Field](/wiki/Matsushima_Air_Field "Matsushima Air Field") of the [Japan Self-Defense Force](/wiki/Japan_Self-Defense_Force "Japan Self-Defense Force") in Miyagi Prefecture received a tsunami warning, and the airbase public address 'Tanoy' was used to give the warning: 'A tsunami is coming evacuate to the third floor.' Shortly after the warning the airbase was struck by the tsunami, flooding the base. There was no loss of life, although the tsunami resulted in damage to all 18 [Mitsubishi F-2](/wiki/Mitsubishi_F-2 "Mitsubishi F-2") fighter jets of the 21st Fighter Training Squadron.[[375]](#cite_note-377)[[376]](#cite_note-378)[[377]](#cite_note-379) Twelve of the aircraft were scrapped, while the remaining six were slated for repair at a cost of 80 billion yen ($1 billion), exceeding the original cost of the aircraft.[[378]](#cite_note-380) After the tsunami, elements of the [Japan Maritime Self-Defense Force](/wiki/Japan_Maritime_Self-Defense_Force "Japan Maritime Self-Defense Force") put to sea without orders and started rescuing those who had been washed out to sea. Tsunami plans were for the Japan Self-Defense Forces assets to be led, directed, and coordinated by local civic governments. However, the earthquake destroyed town halls (the seat of local municipal government), police, and fire services in many places, so the military not only had to respond to but also command rescues.

### Cultural properties

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Monuments_fell_down_by_2011_T%C5%8Dhoku_Earthquake_in_Tokiwa-shrine.JPG/250px-Monuments_fell_down_by_2011_T%C5%8Dhoku_Earthquake_in_Tokiwa-shrine.JPG)](/wiki/File:Monuments_fell_down_by_2011_T%C5%8Dhoku_Earthquake_in_Tokiwa-shrine.JPG)

Damage to a [traditional lantern](/wiki/T%C5%8Dr%C5%8D "Tōrō") at [Tokiwa shrine](/wiki/Tokiwa_Jinja "Tokiwa Jinja") in [Mito City](/wiki/Mito,_Ibaraki "Mito, Ibaraki")

754 [cultural properties](/wiki/Cultural_Properties_of_Japan "Cultural Properties of Japan") were damaged across nineteen prefectures, including five [National Treasures](/wiki/National_Treasures_of_Japan "National Treasures of Japan") (at [Zuigan-ji](/wiki/Zuigan-ji "Zuigan-ji"), [Ōsaki Hachiman-gū](/wiki/%C5%8Csaki_Hachiman-g%C5%AB "Ōsaki Hachiman-gū"), [Shiramizu Amidadō](/wiki/Shiramizu_Amidad%C5%8D "Shiramizu Amidadō"), and [Seihaku-ji](/wiki/Seihaku-ji "Seihaku-ji")); 160 [Important Cultural Properties](/wiki/Important_Cultural_Properties_of_Japan "Important Cultural Properties of Japan") (including at [Sendai Tōshō-gū](/wiki/Sendai_T%C5%8Dsh%C5%8D-g%C5%AB "Sendai Tōshō-gū"), the [Kōdōkan](/wiki/K%C5%8Dd%C5%8Dkan_(Mito) "Kōdōkan (Mito)"), and [Entsū-in](/wiki/Ents%C5%AB-in_(Matsushima) "Entsū-in (Matsushima)"), with its [Western decorative motifs](/wiki/Namban_art "Namban art")); 144 [Monuments of Japan](/wiki/Monuments_of_Japan "Monuments of Japan") (including [Matsushima](/wiki/Matsushima "Matsushima"), [Takata-matsubara](/wiki/Takata-matsubara "Takata-matsubara"), [Yūbikan](/wiki/Y%C5%ABbikan "Yūbikan"), and the [Site of Tagajō](/wiki/Site_of_Tagaj%C5%8D "Site of Tagajō")); six [Groups of Traditional Buildings](/wiki/Groups_of_Traditional_Buildings "Groups of Traditional Buildings"); and four [Important Tangible Folk Cultural Properties](/wiki/Important_Tangible_Folk_Cultural_Properties "Important Tangible Folk Cultural Properties"). Stone monuments at the UNESCO [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"): [Shrines and Temples of Nikkō](/wiki/Shrines_and_Temples_of_Nikk%C5%8D "Shrines and Temples of Nikkō") were toppled.[[379]](#cite_note-381)[[380]](#cite_note-382)[[381]](#cite_note-383) In Tokyo, there was damage to [Koishikawa Kōrakuen](/wiki/Koishikawa_K%C5%8Drakuen "Koishikawa Kōrakuen"), [Rikugien](/wiki/Rikugien_Garden "Rikugien Garden"), [Hamarikyū Onshi Teien](/wiki/Hamarikyu_Gardens "Hamarikyu Gardens"), and the walls of [Edo Castle](/wiki/Edo_Castle "Edo Castle").[[382]](#cite_note-384) Information on the condition of collections held by museums, libraries and archives is still incomplete.[[383]](#cite_note-385) There was no damage to the [Historic Monuments and Sites of Hiraizumi](/wiki/Historic_Monuments_and_Sites_of_Hiraizumi "Historic Monuments and Sites of Hiraizumi") in Iwate Prefecture, and the recommendation for their inscription on the [UNESCO World Heritage List](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Japan "List of World Heritage Sites in Japan") in June was seized upon as a symbol of international recognition and recovery.[[384]](#cite_note-386)

## Aftermath

Main article: [Aftermath of the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami](/wiki/Aftermath_of_the_2011_T%C5%8Dhoku_earthquake_and_tsunami "Aftermath of the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami")

The aftermath of the earthquake and tsunami included a humanitarian crisis and a major economic impact. The tsunami resulted in over 340,000 displaced people in the Tōhoku region, and shortages of food, water, shelter, medicine, and fuel for survivors. In response, the Japanese government mobilized the Self-Defense Forces (under Joint Task Force – Tōhoku, led by Lieutenant General [Eiji Kimizuka](/wiki/Eiji_Kimizuka "Eiji Kimizuka")), while many countries sent search and rescue teams to help search for survivors. Aid organizations in Japan and worldwide responded, with the Japanese Red Cross reporting $1 billion in donations. The economic impact included immediate problems, with industrial production suspended in many factories, and the longer term issue of the cost of rebuilding, which has been estimated at ¥10 trillion ($122 billion). In comparison to the [1995 Great Hanshin earthquake](/wiki/1995_Great_Hanshin_earthquake "1995 Great Hanshin earthquake"), the East Japan earthquake brought serious damage to an extremely wide range.[[385]](#cite_note-387)

The aftermath of the twin disasters left Japan's coastal cities and towns with nearly 25 million tons[*[vague](/wiki/Wikipedia:Vagueness "Wikipedia:Vagueness")*] of debris. In Ishinomaki alone, there were 17 trash collection sites 180 meters (590 ft) long and at least 4.5 meters (15 ft) high. An official in the city's government trash disposal department estimated that it would take three years to empty these sites.[[386]](#cite_note-388)

In April 2015, authorities off the coast of [Oregon](/wiki/Oregon "Oregon") discovered debris that was thought to be from a boat destroyed during the tsunami. The cargo contained [yellowtail amberjack](/wiki/Yellowtail_amberjack "Yellowtail amberjack"), a species of fish that lives off the coast of Japan, still alive. KGW estimates that more than 1 million tons[*[vague](/wiki/Wikipedia:Vagueness "Wikipedia:Vagueness")*] of debris still remain in the Pacific Ocean.[[387]](#cite_note-389)

In February 2016, a memorial was inaugurated by two architects for the victims of the disaster, consisting of a 6.5-square-metre (70 sq ft) structure on a hillside between a temple and a cherry tree in [Ishinomaki](/wiki/Ishinomaki "Ishinomaki").[[388]](#cite_note-390)

The reconstruction of the Tohoku coast was funded and piloted by the Japanese government. Structural adaptation to tsunamis was largely based on building higher [seawalls](/wiki/Seawalls "Seawalls"), although seawalls proved largely ineffective in 2011, and the new seawalls would also not have been high enough.

## Scientific and research response

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Fukushima7.png/500px-Fukushima7.png)](/wiki/File:Fukushima7.png)

Fukushima radiation comparison to other incidents and standards, with graph of recorded radiation levels and specific accident events *(Note: Does not include all radiation readings from Fukushima Daini site.)*

Seismologists anticipated a very large quake would strike in the same place as the [1923 Great Kantō earthquake](/wiki/1923_Great_Kant%C5%8D_earthquake "1923 Great Kantō earthquake")—in the [Sagami Trough](/wiki/Sagami_Trough "Sagami Trough"), southwest of Tokyo.[[60]](#cite_note-National_Geographic-61)[[389]](#cite_note-Achenbach-391) The Japanese government had tracked plate movements since 1976 in preparation for the so-called [Tokai earthquake](/wiki/T%C5%8Dkai_earthquakes "Tōkai earthquakes"), predicted to take place in that region.[[390]](#cite_note-Powell-392) However, occurring as it did 373 km (232 mi) north east of Tokyo, the Tōhoku earthquake came as a surprise to seismologists. While the [Japan Trench](/wiki/Japan_Trench "Japan Trench") was known for creating large quakes, it had not been expected to generate quakes above an 8.0 magnitude.[[389]](#cite_note-Achenbach-391)[[390]](#cite_note-Powell-392) The Headquarters for Earthquake Research Promotion set up by the Japanese government then reassessed the long-term risk of trench-type earthquakes around Japan, and it was announced in November 2011 that research on the [869 Sanriku earthquake](/wiki/869_Sanriku_earthquake "869 Sanriku earthquake") indicated that a similar earthquake with a magnitude of [Mw](/wiki/Seismic_magnitude_scales#Mw "Seismic magnitude scales") 8.4–9.0 would take place off the Pacific coast of northeastern Japan, on average, every 600 years. Also, a tsunami-earthquake with a [tsunami magnitude scales](/wiki/Seismic_scale#Tsunami_magnitude_scales "Seismic scale") (Mt) between 8.6 and 9.0 (Similar to the [1896 Sanriku earthquake](/wiki/1896_Sanriku_earthquake "1896 Sanriku earthquake"), the Mt for the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake was 9.1–9.4) had a 30% chance to occur within 30 years.[[391]](#cite_note-393)[[392]](#cite_note-394)

The quake gave scientists the opportunity to collect a large amount of data to model the seismic events that took place in great detail.[[65]](#cite_note-DW-66) This data is expected to be used in a variety of ways, providing unprecedented information about how buildings respond to shaking, and other effects.[[393]](#cite_note-395) [Gravimetric](/wiki/Gravimetry "Gravimetry") data from the quake have been used to create a model for [increased warning time](/wiki/Earthquake_warning_system "Earthquake warning system") compared to seismic models, as gravity fields travel faster than seismic waves.[[394]](#cite_note-396)

Researchers have also analysed the economic effects of this earthquake and have developed models of the nationwide propagation via interfirm supply networks of the shock that originated in the Tōhoku region.[[395]](#cite_note-Todo-397)[[396]](#cite_note-matous-398)

After the full extent of the disaster was known, researchers soon launched a project to gather all digital material relating to the disaster into an online searchable archive to form the basis of future research into the events during and after the disaster. The Japan Disasters Digital Archive is presented in English and Japanese and is hosted at the Reischauer Institute of Japanese Studies at [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") in Cambridge, Massachusetts.[[397]](#cite_note-399) Some of the first research to come from the archive was a 2014 paper from the Digital Methods Initiative in Amsterdam about patterns of [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter") usage around the time of the disaster.

After the 2011 disaster, the [United Nations International Strategy for Disaster Reduction](/wiki/United_Nations_International_Strategy_for_Disaster_Reduction "United Nations International Strategy for Disaster Reduction") held its [World Conference on Disaster Risk Reduction](/wiki/World_Conference_on_Disaster_Risk_Reduction "World Conference on Disaster Risk Reduction") in Tohoku in March 2015, which produced the [Sendai Framework](/wiki/Sendai_Framework_for_Disaster_Risk_Reduction "Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction") document to guide efforts by international development agencies to act before disasters instead of reacting to them after the fact. At this time Japan's Disaster Management Office (Naikakufu Bosai Keikaku) published a bi-lingual guide in Japanese and English, *Disaster Management in Japan*, to outline the several varieties of natural disaster and the preparations being made for the eventuality of each. In the fall of 2016, Japan's National Research Institute for Earth Science and Disaster Resilience (NIED; Japanese abbreviation, Bosai Kaken; full name Bousai Kagaku Gijutsu Kenkyusho) launched the online interactive "Disaster Chronology Map for Japan, 416–2013" (map labels in Japanese) to display in visual form the location, disaster time, and date across the islands.

The [Japan Trench Fast Drilling Project](/wiki/Japan_Trench_Fast_Drilling_Project "Japan Trench Fast Drilling Project"), a scientific expedition conducted in 2012–2013, drilled ocean-floor boreholes through the fault zone of the earthquake and gathered important data about the rupture mechanism and physical properties of the fault that caused the 2011 earthquake and tsunami.[[398]](#cite_note-smithsonian-400)[[399]](#cite_note-christsci-401)

### Ecological research

The 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami had a great environmental impact on Japan's eastern coast. The rarity and magnitude of the earthquake-tsunami prompted researchers [Jotaro Urabe](/w/index.php?title=Jotaro_Urabe&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jotaro Urabe (page does not exist)"), Takao Suzuki, Tatsuki Nishita, and Wataru Makino to study their immediate ecological impacts on intertidal flat communities at Sendai Bay and the Sanriku Ria coast. Pre- and post-event surveys show a reduction in animal taxon richness and change in taxon composition mainly attributed to the tsunami and its physical impacts. In particular, [sessile](/wiki/Sessility_(motility) "Sessility (motility)") [epibenthic](/wiki/Epibenthic "Epibenthic") animals and endobenthic animals both decreased in taxon richness. Mobile epibenthic animals, such as hermit crabs, were not as affected. Post-surveys also recorded taxa that were not previously recorded before, suggesting that tsunamis have the potential to introduce species and change taxon composition and local community structure. The long term ecological impacts at Sendai Bay and the greater east coast of Japan require further study.[[400]](#cite_note-402)
